Note: The following references are considered to be unofficial sources . They are included to round off this article and may not be authoritative or conclusive.
Kynareth is the goddess of the heavens, the winds, the sea, the elements, and the unseen spirits of the air. [1] [2] [3] She is a member of the Divines and Patron of sailors and travelers. [4] Kynareth is often invoked for auspicious stars at birth and for good fortune in daily life. In some legends, she is the first to agree to Lorkhan 's divine plan to create the mortal plane, and provides the space in the void for its creation. Furthermore, she is associated with rain, a phenomenon that is said not to have occurred before the removal of Lorkhan's divine spark. Some sources describe Spriggans as creations of Kynareth. [5] [6] [7]
The Throat of the World is described as being Kynareth's sacred mountain by the Greybeards , where she breathed life into mankind, and then taught them the thu'um. [8] The Nords and Nedes say that Kyne was the wife of Shor and that upon his death her tears brought the first rain to the world. [9]
Kynareth is the mother of the demigod Morihaus , she is also suspected to have communicated with Saint Alessia through visions of Pelinal 's imminent arrival. [9]
According to followers of the Divines, the Sky Goddess Kyne , worshipped by the Nords as the strongest of the Sky spirits and the widow of Shor, is the Nordic aspect of Kynareth. In Shor's absence following his death, Kyne acts as the head of the Nordic Pantheon, and her role as a warrior Goddess remains the strongest out of the rest of the Nordic Pantheon following Imperial syncretization. [10]
Kyne is credited with 'breathing life into the world' at the top of the Throat of the World, exhaling atop the mountain to create mankind. [11] [12] [13] Rain is said to be Kyne's tears as she mourns the loss of her beloved Shor , since rain is believed not to have appeared until after his divine spark was forcibly removed. [7] It is said Kyne gifted men with the thu'um , either through her daughters or in tandem with Paarthurnax , so they could harness the language of the dragons and use its power to save themselves from the wrath of Alduin and the dragons. [7] [14] [15] :330 Kynareth has a temple in the city of Whiterun ; one of the duties of this temple is to tend the Gildergreen , which is considered sacred to Kynareth's followers. [16]
After imperialization and the loss of independence, some Reachmen embraced the worship of the Eight Divines, including Kynareth, believing that certain aspects of their traditional practices were best left in the past. [17] [18] [19] In the Fourth Era, the Forsworn Reachmen, who strongly opposed their invader's traditions, were observed gathering at the shrine of Kynareth. It remains uncertain whether they were actively worshiping her, however. [20] In the Second Era, a Reachfolk group - Dreadhorn Clan was known to use shamanistic Kyne-magic. [UOL 1]
Khenarthi , God of Winds, the Gatherer of Waters, the Elder Spirit of the Heavens, and the Wind God, is the Khajiiti goddess of weather and the sky, the most powerful of the Sky Spirits, who is believed to have given the first sounds to the world, and is the Khajiiti interpretation of Kynareth . When a Khajiit dies, it is Khenarthi who guides their soul either to Azurah for judgment, or to Llesw'er , the Sands Behind the Stars. And it is her clarion call that will summon the "eternal united spirit of all Khajiit" to defend creation at the end of time. Khajiit credit Khenarthi with leading their ships to safe harbor and their heroes to glory.
Khenarthi, like Alkosh , is said to be able to see across all the Many Paths of Fate .
St. Alessia syncretized elements of the Elven and Nordic Pantheons, forming the Imperial interpretation of Kynareth. [21]
Kynareth is often associated with Zenithar . Craftsmen use natural materials to create, and so one cannot revere Zenithar without acknowledging the power of Kynareth. [22] Canah feathers can be offered to Kynareth at her shrines; in return, the one making the offering will temporarily transform into a Canah. [23]
In contrast to the other Nine Divines, Kynareth has no known Chapel in the great cities of Cyrodiil . [24] Some of Kynareth's faithful have claimed that it is not right to build over nature, Kynareth's work, simply so one can worship her, and so have created altars to the goddess in the Cyrodiilic wilderness, instead. [25]
The island of Betony is considered holy land of Kynareth. This was a factor for Daggerfall 's involvement in the War of Betony . It was also the reason why the conflict escalated to the Battle of Cryngaine Field , which ended the war. [26] Kynareth is the patron deity of Alcaire , Daggerfall and the Wrothgarian Mountains . [27]
Saint Stental was a Breton saint who is best remembered for slaying the demon of Overlook Hill, rescuing the three Sisters of Kynareth from a terrible fate. A memorial, named "The Saint Slays the Leaper", was erected in Stormhaven to honor his courage in 2E 388 , which depicts Stental armed with a sword facing the Leaper demon, depicted as a crouched gargoyle. [28] [29]
Kynareth is an important Divine in Gonfalon Bay . To the locals, she is the patron of sailors and fisherfolk. Every couple of weeks, people celebrate an informal holiday to Kynareth called Angler's Day. Taverns will sell fish-based food at a discount, like Hundred-Year Bisque or Grilled Sweet and Sour Sea Adder . Meanwhile, anglers can drink for free in whatever establishment they can find. [30] The Gonfalon Head Lighthouse is even sacred to the Goddess of Winds and is tended to by an order of priests . [31]
The original Yokudan spirit of the air and the elements is known as Tava , the Bird God. Though the Redguards as a whole worship Tava, the Forebears venerate them under the name Kynareth. In the modern age, Tava has been assimilated into the broader mythology of Kynareth and she is very popular in Hammerfell , particularly among sailors. Shrines of her can be found across most port cities. [32] [33] In the Iliac Bay , the minor regions of Kozanset and Myrkwasa have Kynareth as their patron deity. Their temple, the Temple of Kynareth had many chapters across the regions. [27]
The air spirits Amaro, Pina, and Tallatha, are acknowledged as the Fingers of Kynareth by the Skeffington Coven . [34]
The Kothringi , the indigenous humans of Black Marsh, are known to have worshipped Kynareth, alongside Mara and Dibella as one of the "mothers in the Around-Us". [UOL 2]
Some fledgling gryphons are said to have been sent to Nirn by Kynareth so they'd find companions that could help them value both land and sky equally. [35] Some Quasigriffs are said to have been touched by Kynareth. It is said that, while astride, their rider will never feel the burn of the sun and will always have the wind at their back. [36]
The Boots of the Crusader (also known as the Boots of Kynareth) were created by Kynareth. The wearer will not be attacked by creatures of the forest. Sir Juncan of the original Knights died while searching for the boots. The pilgrim visited the Shrine of Kynareth, and was told the location of the "Grove of Trials" by the high priestess (later to become Sir Avita Vesnia). Kynareth tested the pilgrim by sending the Forest Guardian, a giant bear , to attack them. When the pilgrim didn't fight back, having reverence for nature, Kynareth allowed them entrance to the grotto where the boots were hidden. The boots were needed to retrieve the Mace of the Crusader; with them, the wearer's faith is strengthened and they can cross the void of doubt. [6]
The Lord's Mail (also called the Armor of Morihaus or the Gift of Kynareth) is an artifact given to mortals by Kynareth , one of the Eight Divines . It is an ancient plated adamantium or mithril cuirass of unsurpassable quality, considered to be heavy armor. It grants the wearer the power to absorb or regenerate health, resist the effects of spells, and resist or even cure poison. It is said that whenever Kynareth deigns the wearer unworthy, the Lord's Mail will be taken away and hidden for the next chosen one. The Mail was originally owned during the Merethic Era by the bull Morihaus , a demigod and early cultural hero of the Cyro-Nordics who was closely associated with Kynareth and the Lord . The cuirass was rumored to have later been taken from Morihaus by Kynareth herself due to his arrogance. The cuirass is considered an Imperial relic, and is accepted as a uniform in the Imperial Legion . Indeed, the armor has been known to bear the red dragon sigil of the Third Empire on its breastplate.
The Ring of Kynareth is an enchanted silver ring with a sapphire gemstone associated with the goddess Kynareth . It possesses the ability to influence the weather , causing rainstorms to arise when worn while fishing in temperate lakes and streams.
The Ring of the Wind is an enchanted ring from the province of Elsweyr . It is a legendary artifact associated with Kynareth , the Goddess of the Winds, or Zenithar , the God of Work and Commerce. Very little information is known about the ring, other than the fact that it grants the wearer great speed.
The planet Kynareth , sometimes called KYNRT, is one of the Planets in the skies of Mundus , and according to the Warrior-Poet, Vivec , it is one of the eight worlds known to the Dwemer . There are no known satellites orbiting around it. As with all astral bodies seen in the skies above Nirn and their corresponding deities, the planet Kynareth is believed to be the plane of the goddess Kynareth as well as the goddess herself, as seen from the mortal plane. It is said to actually be a different plane of existence in its own right, infinite in size and mass, with its appearance as a sphere being only a visual phenomenon caused by mortal mental stress. According to some accounts, the mortal realms were formed by the Aedra out of Creatia .

Note: The following references are considered to be unofficial sources . They are included to round off this article and may not be authoritative or conclusive.
Mara , also known as Mother Mara, [1] [2] [3] [4] Mother Mild, [5] and the Divine Mother, [1] is the Goddess of Love, Fertility, Agriculture, [6] and Compassion. [7] She is also acclaimed as the Mother-Goddess, [8] and is one of the Divines . [9]
In the Merethic Era , Mara was originally worshipped simply as a fertility goddess, [10] but the sphere of the Divine later extended to agriculture and love. Nir, the female principle of the cosmos credited as the mother of creation, is occasionally associated with Mara as a result of her role as the goddess of fertility and agriculture. [6] As a result, priests of Mara credit her with giving birth to creation. [11] Mara is considered a near universal deity as the Divine is featured in the pantheons of all the races of the continent of Tamriel save the Dunmer and Argonians . [8]
According to her clergy, it was Mara who first gave birth to Creation. She then pledged to watch over mortals as her children. They also believe mortals learned to love each other from her love for all of them. [11] According to the creation myth presented in the Anuad , Mara and the Aedra (gods) were born from the mingled blood of Anu and Padomay , the good and evil primal forces, respectively, and therefore have a capacity for both good and evil, in contrast to the Daedra , who were born from the blood of Padomay, and thus are only evil. [13]
The creation myth of the Khajiit acclaims Mara as the Mother Cat and describes the Divine as the daughter of Ahnurr (Anu) and Fadomai (Padomay). [14]
The formation of Akatosh , the Dragon God of Time, from the mingled blood of the brothers Anu and Padomay facilitated the formation of Mara and the gods as they learnt to structure themselves. [15] Shezarr's Song, a Cyrodilic creation myth, acclaims the creation of Mundus to the sacrifice of Mara and the gods whose sacrifices were embodied as eponymous planets. [16] [17]
Mara and the gods were subsequently bound to the Earth Bones as a result. [16] The moons of Masser and Secunda are claimed to be the embodiment of the lovers, a mortal woman named Mara and Shandar , respectively, as created by the Divine. [12] When Magnus created his daughter Londa-Vera , he drew inspiration from other divinities and bestowed upon her the smile of Mara. [18]
Different cultures attribute different relationships between Mara and the other gods. The Nords of Skyrim consider Mara a handmaiden of Kyne and concubine of Shor (Lorkhan). [6] However, the Bretons, Altmer, and Bosmer consider Mara to be the wife of Akatosh or Auriel to the Elves. [10] [19] [20] Other faiths acclaim Mara as the wife of Lorkhan or concubine of Akatosh, or, in some cases, concubine of both. [8] In Ark'ay The God, Mymophonus the scribe claims that Mara deified Arkay, the God of Birth and Death, so to allow him to teach mortals the necessity of life and death. [2] In the First Era, the Divine punished Sai , God of Luck, and deprived him of his body for his failure to uphold his pact with Ebonarm , God of War, to spread luck throughout Tamriel in the gods' absence. [21] The Warp in the West , also known as the Miracle of Peace, in 3E 417 is acclaimed as the work of Mara, Akatosh, and Stendarr. [22] It is unknown if Mara successfully destroyed the Skull of Corruption , a daedric artifact, in 4E 201. [23]
On occasions, she would act through intermediaries to ensure that the spirits of the deceased would be united together also in death. [24] There are also documented cases of Mara intervening more directly, enabling the departed to briefly return as ghosts to share final moments with their loved ones and guide them beyond the mortal realm. This intervention extended even to situations involving individuals from different races with distinct beliefs about the afterlife. Notably, Mara facilitated the reunion of Nuralanya, a Corelanya Altmer , and an Orc named Gurlak in the afterlife. [25] [26] The clergy of Mara supported this belief, teaching that even couples from different backgrounds, such as an Altmer and a Reachfolk , would not enter death alone. [27]
As Goddess of Love, Mara is the bearer of love, [28] and shines the light of love on all mortals. [29] Mara gave life to mortals so that they may love. [30] The gift of love was bestowed upon mortals by the Divine so to honor Mara, [30] and allow them to understand the true nature of the Divines. [28] Mara teaches that love provides nourishment and life to all mortals. [28] The Divine commands mortals to "live soberly and peacefully. Honor your parents, and preserve the peace and security of home and family". [31] Mara's Benevolence is eternal and those who offer their love to the Divine will never be forsaken. [32] The Warmth of Mara shapes the destiny of mortals and can be felt across Tamriel. [32] Priests of Mara teach that the Divine is omnipresent and her love and affection can be found in the heart of all mortals, thus linking them to Mara. [33] The holy truth declares that love creates life and therefore those who love create more life. [30]
Mara is also the patron of marriage , [28] and the act of union is carried out at her shrines and temples. [34] [35] The Divine teaches that the spark of union between two mortals comes from within and is not based on appearance. [28] The oaths and vows of marriage create life from love and are respected in the name of Mara. [30] The act of union is confirmed upon taking the Pledge of Mara and Mara does not place any restrictions on marriage, thus all may marry, irrespective of gender and race, and unite their souls in the holy union. [1] Mara makes her will known to her priests through visions, [36] and commands them to aid those whose love needs guidance. [24] The priesthood of Mara strive to unite all mortals and are intolerant of those who are intolerant of others. [37] Mara's Prayer from the Doomed is a prayer recited in desperate situations. [38]
Heart's Day is a holiday celebrated throughout Tamriel on the 16th of Sun's Dawn and serves as the central day of the week-long Heart’s Week festival, honoring love wherever it is found, among friends, lovers, and even passing strangers. [39] [40] [41] Ironically, Heart's Day originated as the Summoning Day dedicated to Sanguine , the Daedric Prince of Debauchery. [41] [42] As newer religions rose to prominence, they appropriated the holiday and sanitized Sanguine’s influence in order to strengthen devotion to their own traditions. [42] Since then, it has been predominantly associated with Mara. [43] [44] [45]
The majority of the population of the province of Black Marsh venerate the Hist , and, in the Second Era, worship among the Argonians of Black Marsh of Mara and the gods was limited to a small minority of those who had assimilated to the culture of the Empire. [46] Imperial missionary efforts in the province in the Third Era were unsuccessful as missionaries died from local diseases, such as at Umpholo, where a Temple of Mara was constructed. [47] Mara is known to have been worshipped as a member of the pantheon of the Kothringi , the indigenous humans of Black Marsh, and was considered one of their three "mothers in the Around-Us", as well as Dibella and Kynareth . [UOL 1]
The Imperials of the province of Cyrodiil venerate Mara and the Divine is considered part of the Cyrodilic pantheon. [8] A Benevolence of Mara, a cathedral dedicated to the Divine, was located in the Imperial City , the capital of the province, in the late First Era , under the Reman Dynasty . [48] In the 5th century of the Second Era, the Imperial City was also home to a Yokudan chapel in the Market District which contained a shrine to Morwha shaped like a beehive. [49] A Temple of Mara, and later Great Chapel of Mara, is known to have been located at Bravil in the Second and Third Eras, respectively. [50] [51] [52] In the late Third Era , wayshrines dedicated to the Divine can be found throughout the countryside. [53] At this time, the office of Archbishop of Mara is attested. [47] A wayshrine of Mara northwest of the city of Anvil was destroyed during the Oblivion Crisis in 3E 433. [53] [54] In the same year, the Great Chapel of Mara at Bravil was desecrated by Aurorans , daedra in the service of Meridia , and several of its priests were killed. [55] [52]
Although Ayleids followed the Daedra, the Aedra of Elven pantheon were still widely revered, with probably a majority of the Ayleids continuing to pay them homage, but cults devoted to the various Daedric Princes sprang up across Cyrodiil, tolerated and then celebrated. [56]
The worship of Mara in the province of Hammerfell is divided between the Crowns, who represent the conservatives, and Forebears, who represent the cosmopolitan Redguards. [57] Mara is known to both Crowns and Forebears as Morwha , a fertility goddess, however, the names Morwha and Mara are used interchangeably among the Forebears. [58] Morwha is considered the favorite wife of Akatosh or Ruptga, to the Forebears and Crowns, respectively. [58] The goddess is always depicted with four arms so that she may "grab more husbands". [57] Mara is worshipped throughout Hammerfell, including the Island of Stros M'Kai , [58] and is the patron deity of the regions of Antiphyllos , [59] Ayasofya , [60] Ephesus , [61] and Ykalon . [62] In the late Third Era, similarly to High Rock, the Benevolence of Mara was responsible for the administration of temples dedicated to Mara and the Maran Knights was also active in the province at this time. [37] [63] A Conservatorium of Morwha was located in the city of Hegathe in the late First Era. [64]
Mara, a member of the pantheon of the Bretons , [8] is the patron deity of several regions of High Rock, such as Anticlere , Betony , Glenumbra Moors , and Northmoor . [65] It is said that villages which have clement weather year-round are protected by the Mother Goddess. [UOL 2] In Anticlere, the 21st of Last Seed was known as Appreciation Day , an ancient holiday of thanksgiving for a bountiful harvest. It was considered a holy and contemplative day devoted to Mara, the goddess-protector of the region. [66]
Nedic sculptures depicted coupled idols of an Elven Mara embracing a Mannish Lorkhan, seen as early evidence of Aldmeri culture being welcomed with open arms by the Manmer . [67] In the late Third Era, temples dedicated to the Divine were administered by the Benevolence of Mara , a religious organization dedicated to Mara and her teachings, and could be found in major urban centers throughout the province. The Benevolence of Mara was led by a patriarch and was allied to the Temple of Stendarr , a religious organization dedicated to Stendarr and his teachings. [37] The Maran Knights were a controversial knightly order dedicated to Mara and the protection of her temples. Some within the priesthood of Mara called for the disbandment of the order, while others argued the order was necessary to create peace. [63]
The Dunmer of Morrowind largely neglect worship of Mara and the Divines, however, in the late Third Era, worship of Mara and the Divines was practiced by a small minority of Dunmer on the Island of Vvardenfell. Worship of the Divine in Vvardenfell in the late Third Era was largely confined to its non-Dunmer population who prayed at shrines operated by the Imperial Cult , a missionary group, which combined worship of the Divines and provided services for all the Divines at shrines found at Imperial forts. [68] A shrine to Mara and the Divines was also located in the Royal Palace of Mournhold during this time. [69] Following the collapse of the Tribunal Temple in 3E 427, worshippers of Mara and the Divines formed the majority of Hlaalu and Vvardenfell districts. [70] In the Second Era, during the Three Banners War , shrines of Mara were located in the cities of Davon's Watch and Mournhold. [34]
During the Interregnum, it was customary to decorate the steets of Markarth for the celebrations of the Heart's Week . The decoartions included banners, blue floral patterns and hanging Amulets of Mara, along with pink and red flowers associated with Dibella. [71] Circa 2E 582 , two Reachfolk clans, Eagleseers and Six-Fords were to hold a handfasting upon an altar of Mara at Hroldan Ring to unite the two clans. [72] One of the direwolves kept in the Shrine of the Hunt-Father 's grounds was named after Mara. [73] Some Reacholk marriage traditions share similarities to Mara worshipping Bretons and Snow Elves . [UOL 3]
After imperialization and the loss of independence, some Reachmen embraced the worship of the Eight Divines, including Mara, believing that certain aspects of their traditional practices were best left in the past. [74] [75] [76] As of 4E 201 several Reachfolk accept marriage under Mara. [35] Spirits of Briarhearts , whose original hearts were defiled are unable to pass to the afterlife, [77] [78] unless the original heart of the deceased Briarheart is purified at the shrine of Mara and properly buried. [79]
The Divine is also worshipped as a member of the Nordic pantheon. [8] The Sacred Wolf was one of the most revered Atmoran animal totems, associated by later Nords with the goddess Mara, [80] who was known as Mother Wolf. [81] [82] In the Second Era, Mara was associated with the Ice Wolves that inhabited the mouth of the White River in the northeast of the province. [83] Wooden figures of Mara depict a naked woman partially clothed in wolf pelts who bears fangs of her own, similar to Atmoran stone carvings. [84]
Worship of Mara in the province of Skyrim was widespread in the Fourth Era and shrines dedicated to the Divine could be found both in the wilderness and in urban centers, such as at the Temple of the Divines in Solitude , [85] and at the Temple of Mara in Riften . [86] Shrines dedicated to Mara usually have flower-spouting fertility statuettes. [87] A pond in the hold of Eastmarch was named after Mara. [88] Some argued the return of the dragons in 4E 201 was a sign from Mara signaling her displeasure with the people of Skyrim. [11]
To Nordic traditionalists, Mara takes a lesser role in the pantheon, considered to be Kyne's handmaiden, and one of Shor's concubines. [6] [UOL 4] Her worship tends to be more emphasized in areas with a large Imperial influence. Amulets of Mara are frequently worn to indicate that someone is looking to marry, as life is hard and full of hardship in Skyrim courtships tend to be very short. Marriages are often held in Riften's Temple of Mara. [11]
Mara is worshipped in the High Elven pantheon. [20] An Altmer legend tells that Nenamil, the Archon of Song, once embarked on a pilgrimage to see Mara's beauty in person, believing that the greatest love he could feel was love for the mother of creation. His journey eventually took him to what is now known as Archon's Grove , where he found a flower of surpassing beauty, the so-called Rose-of-Archon. Upon seeing the flower, he realized that Mara was more than just a being: she was beauty incarnate, found in every exquisite thing. From then on, Nenamil would preach Mara's beauty and the Rose-of-Archon would honor and represent her. Roses-of-Archon are collected by the Sapiarchs for the Divine Reverence to Mara, a ceremony that lasts for at least fifty-eight days. [89]
During the Three Banners War in the Second Era , a Temple of Mara located in the town of Bravil , Cyrodiil, was home to several holy relics of Mara, including a chalice, phylactery, dagger, and circlet. The Chalice of Mara was alleged to have been a wedding gift from Mara to St. Alessia , founder of the Alessian Empire , at her wedding to Morihaus . [90] In 2E 582 , the Sphinxmoth Bandits, a group of bandits operating from the neighboring Fort Sphinxmoth , looted the temple of its valuables, including the relics. The bandits were defeated by the Vestige and the relics were subsequently returned to the temple. [91] In 3E 427 , on the Island of Vvardenfell , the Divine appeared before the Nerevarine as an apparition in the form of a Breton woman named Ama Nin and gifted the Nerevarine her blouse and skirt. [92] Some claim the Everflow Ewer was created by Mara. [93]
Of the Crusader's Relics , it is unknown whether Mara created the Cuirass or Greaves of the Crusader. The Crusader's Relics are a set of artifacts created by the Divines in the early First Era for Pelinal Whitestrake , the Divine Crusader, to allow him to defeat and banish Umaril the Unfeathered , an Ayleid Sorcerer King. Regardless, Pelinal, despite his victory over Umaril, was slain and the Crusader's Relics were scattered and lost for thousands of years. [94] The Cuirass of the Crusader was held by the Wyrm of Elynglenn in Cyrodiil until recovered by Sir Amiel Lannus, a member of the chivalric order the Knights of the Nine, in 3E 111. [95] The cuirass was stored in the undercroft of the Priory of the Nine, where it lay until recovered by the Hero of Kvatch in 3E 433. [96] The Greaves of the Crusader were recovered by Sir Berich Vlindrel and were buried with him in the Vlindrel family tomb, but was later recovered by Sir Roderic of Wayrest and his squire Lathon, who gifted the greaves to the Hero of Kvatch. [97]
Mara's Blouse is a piece of clothing associated with the Goddess of Love, Mara . It is a red blouse imbued with magical properties that protect the wearer from the effects of hostile spells.
Mara's Skirt is a piece of clothing associated with the Goddess of Love, Mara . It is a red skirt imbued with magical properties that improve the vitality and health of the wearer.
Mother Mild's Sickle of Absolution is a lesser known artifact associated with goddess Mara . Little is known of the sickle or of the practices it was tied to, though it was said to have been associated with blood rites long before such rituals became linked with sinister or macabre connotations. According to Sanguine , Mara did not always harvest crops alone.
The planet Mara , sometimes called MHARA, is one of the Planets in the skies of Mundus , and according to the Warrior-Poet, Vivec , is one of the eight worlds known to the Dwemer . Mara is Zenithar 's satellite and is orbiting around it, while Dibella is orbiting around Mara. As with all astral bodies seen in the skies above Nirn and their corresponding deities, the planet Mara is believed to be the plane of the goddess Mara as well as the goddess herself, as seen from the mortal plane. It is said to actually be a different plane of existence in its own right, infinite in size and mass, with its appearance as a sphere being only a visual phenomenon caused by mortal mental stress. According to some accounts, the mortal realms were formed by the Aedra out of Creatia .

Note: The following references are considered to be unofficial sources . They are included to round off this article and may not be authoritative or conclusive.
Alkosh • Baan Dar • Jode • Jone • Khenarthi • Mara • Riddle'Thar • S'rendarr
Stendarr , also known as Stendarr the Steadfast, [1] is the God of Mercy, Charity, Well-Earned Luck, [2] and Justice. [3] He is also acclaimed as the God of Compassion, [4] [5] and Righteous Rule by Might and Merciful Forbearance. [6] Stendarr is the Apologist of Men , [7] patron of the Imperial Legion , [8] magistrates, rulers, and knights errant, [9] and one of the Divines . [10]
Stendarr was originally worshipped as Stuhn , God of Ransom, shield-thane of Shor , but later developed into the current form of worship as a deity of compassion or, sometimes, righteous rule. [7] He is worshipped throughout Tamriel and is considered part of the Imperial , Breton , Altmer , Bosmer and Khajiit pantheons. [11] [7]
The Imperials of the province of Cyrodiil venerate Stendarr and the Divine is considered part of the Cyrodilic pantheon. [7] A Chapel of Stendarr is known to have been located in the city of Chorrol in the Second and Third Eras. [12] [13] In the late Third Era, wayshrines dedicated to the Divine can be found throughout the countryside of the province. [13] A statue of Stendarr was featured in the Arboretum district of the Imperial City , the capital of Cyrodiil, in the Second and Third Eras. [12] [13]
Although Ayleids followed the Daedra, the Aedra of Elven pantheon were still widely revered, with probably a majority of the Ayleids continuing to pay them homage, but cults devoted to the various Daedric Princes sprang up across Cyrodiil, tolerated and then celebrated. [14]
Stendarr is worshipped as S'rendarr, the Runt, among the Khajiit of Elsweyr and is regarded as the son of Ahnurr and Fadomai . [15] According to the Khajiit, Ahnurr and Fadomai acclaimed S'rendarr as God of Mercy as he was the weakest child. [15] As God of Mercy, the Khajiit associate S'rendarr with compassion, charity and justice. [16] A Shrine of the Consummate S'rendarr was located in Jodewood in the Second Era until its priests were exiled for refusing to acknowledge the edicts proclaimed after the adoption of the Riddle'Thar Epiphany, causing the temple to fall into ruin and become occupied by bandits. [12]
The majority of the population of Morrowind do not venerate Stendarr, [17] however, in the late Third Era, worship of Stendarr and the Divines was practiced by a small minority of Dunmer on the Island of Vvardenfell . [18] Worship of the Divine in Vvardenfell in the late Third Era was largely confined to its non-Dunmer population who could pray at shrines operated by the Imperial Cult , a missionary group, which combined worship of the Divines and provided services for all the Divines at shrines found at Imperial forts. [18] A shrine to Stendarr and the Divines was also located in the Royal Palace of Mournhold during this time. [19] Members of the Imperial Cult were said to "serve Stendarr" by taking on roles of protection. [8] Iulus Truptor, a member of the Imperial Cult, was called the "Stendarr Savant". [8] Another cult member claimed to have received visions from Stendarr guiding her to lost treasures. [8]
Following the collapse of the Tribunal Temple in 3E 427, worshippers of Stendarr and the Divines formed the majority of Hlaalu and Vvardenfell districts. [20] In the Second Era, during the Alliance War , a shrine to Stendarr, in the name of Stuhn, was located at the city of Mournhold. [12]
The ancient Nords worshipped Stendarr in the role of Stuhn associated with the totem of the whale.[ citation needed ] Stuhn was considered the God of Ransom and Justice, who taught the Nords the benefits of taking hostages. Stuhn (sometimes also called Stendarr), [21] :152 was said to be brother of Tsun and both were shield-thanes of Shor who defended him against foreign gods, who would fell Tsun. [7] When Alessia overthrew the Ayleid Empire she combined the Nordic and Elven Pantheons creating a new Pantheon of the Eight Divines. Though it was controversial in some parts of Skyrim, most Nords identify with Stendarr over Stuhn, whereas by contrast many still refer to Kynerath as her aspect of Kyne.[ citation needed ]
In the Fourth Era, shrines to Stendarr could be found throughout the province of Skyrim , in the wilderness and in urban centers, such as at the Temple of the Divines in Solitude . [22] The Vigil of Stendarr , a holy order founded after the Oblivion Crisis in 3E 433, had a chapter-house in Skyrim in the Fourth Era which was located south of the city of Dawnstar . [22] The Vigilants of Stendarr followed the teachings of Stendarr, provided healing services to the population of Skyrim, and engaged in combat with Abominations. [22] The chapter-house was destroyed in 4E 201 and its occupants, including the Keeper of the Vigil and head of the chapter in Skyrim, were killed by the Volkihar , the predominant vampire clan in the province. [23] The holy order also utilized a watchtower known as Stendarr's Beacon during this time. [22] A shrine dedicated to Stendarr and the Divines was located at Fort Frostmoth on the Island of Solstheim in the late Third Era. [24]
After imperialization and the loss of independence, some Reachmen embraced the worship of the Eight Divines, including Stendarr, believing that certain aspects of their traditional practices were best left in the past. [25] [26] [27]
Stendarr is the patron deity of several regions in the Iliac Bay , including Alik'r , Bhoriane , Mournoth , Phrygias , Santaki and Urvaius , and is venerated by Redguards and Bretons alike. [28] [29] Temples dedicated to the Divine could be found throughout the Iliac Bay during the late Third Era and were administered by the Temple of Stendarr , a religious organization dedicated to Stendarr and his teachings, which offered healing and training to the population of the Iliac Bay. [30] Only healers of the temples of Stendarr offered their services to both worshippers and heretics. [31] The Temple of Stendarr was led by a patriarch and was allied to the Benevolence of Mara , a religious organization dedicated to Mara and her teachings. [30] The Crusaders , also known as the Knights of Stendarr, was a knightly order dedicated to Stendarr which protected his temples against its adversaries, such as the Citadel of Ebonarm . [32] Stendarr is considered the most popular Divine among Redguard knights. [33]
Some Breton sources link Shandar to Stendarr. Depending on the account, Shandar is described as a distinct et'Ada, a mortal, a fictional figure, or an aspect of Stendarr. In other tales, he is said to be related to Stendarr, variously portrayed as a twin brother, a rival, or a son. Shandar represents war and cruelty, standing in contrast to Stendarr's mercy; however, in many tales, he ultimately rejects these ways and becomes merciful, becoming almost akin to a saint of Stendarr. [UOL 1]
The Sect of Harmonious Masters of Lillandril was a sect dedicated to the teachings of Stendarr that emerged during the Second Era in the city of Lillandril. [34] The sect abhorred violence, offered healing and promulgated knowledge of healing spells to the general population. [35] The veneration of Stendarr is frowned upon by a minority of Elves who consider the god unworthy of the worship of the Children of Aldmeris because of his role as the Apologist of Men. [35] Aritanwe of Lillandril, a notable member of the sect, refuted criticism of the veneration of Stendarr among Elves in Rituals of the Harmonious Masters. [35]
According to the creation myth presented in the Anuad, Stendarr and the aedra (gods) were born from the mingled blood of Anu and Padomay , the good and evil primal forces, respectively, and therefore have a capacity for both good and evil, in contrast to the daedra, who were born from the blood of Padomay, and thus are only evil. [36] The formation of Akatosh , the Dragon God of Time, from the mingled blood of the brothers Anu and Padomay facilitated the formation of Stendarr and the gods as they learnt to structure themselves. [37] Shezarr's Song, a Cyrodiilic creation myth, acclaims the creation of Mundus to the sacrifice of Stendarr and the gods whose sacrifices were embodied as eponymous planets. [38] [39] Stendarr and the gods were subsequently bound to the Earth Bones as a result. [38] It is said that the Divine accompanied Emperor Tiber Septim in his later years in the early Third Era. [7] The Warp in the West , also known as the Miracle of Peace, in 3E 417 is attributed to Stendarr, Mara and Akatosh . [40]
Stendarr, through his priests, resolutes, and templars, makes his will known to the mortals of Tamriel, [42] and commands them to "be kind and generous to the people of Tamriel. Protect the weak, heal the sick, and give to the needy". [43] Stendarr offers mercy to all mortals and he welcomes heretics, the afflicted, the hopeless and the forgotten. [44] The Divine cherishes and protects all mortals regardless of whether they acknowledge him or not and he does not distinguish between worshippers and heretics. [17] The priests of Stendarr act as a conduit and he provides guidance and assistance to mortals through them. [44] Mortals who open their heart and soul to Stendarr's mercy and seek his benevolence are healed and gain understanding of the love Stendarr holds for all mortals, particularly those less fortunate. [35] [44] He also offers help in the form of healing as Stendarr can mend any wound, stay any disease, and soothe any broken soul. [44]
Stendarr bestowed upon mortals the gift of magic and the ability to employ it, [35] with which mortals can seek Stendarr's wisdom through the use of restoration magic in his name. [44] Invocation of Stendarr grants the wielder the ability to cloak themselves in a righteous aura of blessed light, which has been adapted by the priests and resolutes of Stendarr to form either, a piercing beam resembling a spear, a form of armor or shield, or for use as a tool for healing. [34] However, the mercy of Stendarr does not extend to the enemies of mortals, who are referred to as Abominations, and the Divine considers them abhorrent and unnatural, and deserving of extermination without mercy. [42] Vinicius Imbrex, Archbishop of Chorrol, defined four kinds of Abominations - daedra, lycanthropes, the undead and vampires - in The Four Abominations. [42] Stendarr is known as the Divine who "suffers Men to read". [45]
Stendarr commands all mortals to make themselves vulnerable to his will and follow his precepts:
The Gauntlets of the Crusader, one of the Crusader's Relics , was created by Stendarr in the early First Era and granted to Pelinal Whitestrake , the Divine Crusader, to allow him to defeat and banish Umaril the Unfeathered , an Ayleid Sorcerer King. Pelinal, despite his victory over Umaril, was slain and the Crusader's Relics were scattered and lost for thousands of years. [46] The gauntlets were later recovered by Sir Casimir, a Knight of the Nine , in the early Third Era and remained in his possession until 3E 139. [46] The murder of a beggar at the hands of Sir Casimir in the Chapel of Stendarr in Chorrol in 3E 139 led Stendarr to deem Sir Casimir unworthy of his artifact and the gauntlets fell to the floor where they lay until coming into the possession of the Hero of Kvatch in 3E 433. [47]
Stendarr's Hammer is a hammer rumored to have once been wielded by Stendarr , the God of Justice. One of his other titles, the God of Righteous Might, is fitting as the hammer is very heavy. It is made of ebony and sapphire , which can also be used to further temper it The weapon is enchanted to drain or damage the health of those it strikes, at the cost of the wielder's stamina.
The Ring of Stendarr's Mercy is a ring that was linked to the Amulet of Kings and Sancre Tor , the ring served as a key to a ward of Stendarr protecting the Amulet in 2E 582 . It was used by the Vestige to retrieve the Amulet alongside Sai Sahan . [48]
Widowill is a suit of armor imbued with Stendarran holy protections. It was created when a soldier's widow in an Imperial village decided to take her husband's arms against a necromancer group that had set up nearby and had started grave robbing and kidnapping people. Stendarr noticed the woman's selflessness and blessed the armor with protections that reduced the attacking necromancers to dust. [49]
The planet Stendarr , sometimes called THENDR, is one of the Planets in the skies of Mundus , and according to the Warrior-Poet, Vivec , it is one of the eight worlds known to the Dwemer . Stendarr is Julianos ' satellite and is orbiting around it. As with all astral bodies seen in the skies above Nirn and their corresponding deities, the planet Stendarr is believed to be the plane of the god Stendarr as well as the god himself, as seen from the mortal plane. It is said to actually be a different plane of existence in its own right, infinite in size and mass, with its appearance as a sphere being only a visual phenomenon caused by mortal mental stress. According to some accounts, the mortal realms were formed by the Aedra out of Creatia .

Alkosh • Baan Dar • Jode • Jone • Khenarthi • Mara • Riddle'Thar • S'rendarr
Zenithar , the God of Work and Commerce, the Provider of our Ease, [1] is one of the Divines . [2] [3] [4] The Trader God is frequently seen as the same deity as the Bosmeri god Z'en . [5] In the Empire , however, Zenithar is considered a more cultivated god of merchants and middle nobility, being the deity of wealth, labor, commerce, communication, [5] [6] and community. [UOL 1]
He has strong ties to Cyrodiil and High Rock . [5] The introduction of the worship of Zenithar was largely tolerated in Hammerfell due to his similarities to a Yokudan agricultural deity. [7] His worshippers say that, despite his mysterious origins, Zenithar is the god 'that will always win'. [5] His priests teach that the path to peace and prosperity is through earnest work and honest profit, not through war, bloodshed, or theft. [8] Zenithar is seen as a warrior god, but one who is restrained and reserved in times of peace. [9] [10]
He is thought to be associated with Kynareth , [11] as well as a large blue star sometimes seen in the skies of Tamriel . [12] It's also said that he is most in touch with the mortal world, and that he created the Mace of the Crusader for Pelinal Whitestrake to help him defeat Umaril the Unfeathered . [13]
Zenithar appeared to the Nerevarine during their service to the Imperial Cult , as a Redguard named Jon Hawker, and after he had been freed, the Nerevarine received his gauntlets, Zenithar's Warning and Zenithar's Wiles. [14]
Temples devoted to him are sometimes called Resolutions . Worship of Zenithar is typically more prevalent in some geographic areas, such as Leyawiin , Totambu , and Kambria . [3]
A blacksmith's anvil is his symbol, representing labor and production. The Daggerfall Merchant's Guild is known to award small golden anvils in Zenithar's name to upstanding members. [15] Worshippers of Zenithar often keep small devotional anvils in their homes and pewter scale weights engraved with a blue star in the coin purses for financial luck in trying times. [16] [17]
One form of an offering to Zenithar is to burn crops at a shrine of him to offer thanks for prosperity. [18] Worshippers also use special prayer beads to call forth good luck and success in all types of negotiations. [19] During special celebrations dedicated to Zenithar, many cities all around Tamriel were decorated with imagery of the blue star, coins and crops. [20]
Zentihar is also venerated on the isle of Solstice , the seat of Clan Corelanya . His worship was introduced to Sunport by Imperial traders from Leyawiin. As the patron of commerce and craftsmanship, he has gained a devoted following among Sunport's merchants and traders. [21]
During the Interregnum, the free city of Markarth was one of many cities decorated with Zenithar images and devotional objects during special celebrations dedicated to the god. [22] After Imperialization and the loss of independence, some Reachmen embraced the worship of the Eight Divines, including Zenithar, believing that certain aspects of their traditional practices were best left in the past. [23] [24] [25] As of then a shrine of Zenithar was found close to the Four Skull Lookout . [26] [27]
Some Dunmer followers of the Eight Divines considered the sacred toil, fortitude, and triumph in the face of hardship spoken of by the prophet Veloth as virtues best embodied by Zenithar. [28]
A holy relic of Zenithar, that was made of unremarkable iron, by a pious smith, Zenithar honored the smiths work by turning the anvil into gold. The anvil would then fall under the care of the priests of Zenithar's Abbey, which later fell into ruins by avarice. [29] The Redguard resolute, Daluion traveled to the ruined Zenithar's Abbey to find the long-lost Golden Anvil and learn the story of the abbey's arbitrator, Gavos Douar . With the help of an adventurer , they were able to bypass the monsters that inhabited the ruin and recover the sigil keys to the adytum on the east side of the abbey.
They found the decrepit and corroded Golden Anvil on the temple's main altar as well as an elderly Gavos Douar, who tried to preserve the anvil for many years. Before Zenithar's faith was forever lost in the abbey and the anvil, Daluion blessed it with the adventurer's toil and it brought holy light back to the Golden Anvil. [30] Shortly after its discovery, Daluion returned to the temple in Leyawiin and informed the Primate of the anvil's location. The Knights of Iron were deployed to recover it and cleanse the abbey. [29]
The Mace of the Crusader (also known as the Mace of Zenithar) was created by Zenithar. Those it strikes are burnt by holy flames, and it has the ability to turn the undead. According to legend, when Pelinal Whitestrake was slain, a messenger carried the mace to the settlement of Leyawiin . Centuries later, the master craftsman Saint Kaladas built the Great Chapel of Zenithar to honor the legend. When Kaladas died and was entombed in the Chapel, those who prayed to him began to receive visions of the mace. In the vision, the mace can be seen in the distance, separated by a void. The void symbolizes doubt, and an inscription on the tomb says to "walk in the faith". Sir Ralvas of the original Knights attempted hundreds of times to reach the mace, but failed each time. The pilgrim later discovered that the Boots of the Crusader are needed to cross the void; Zenithar has close ties to Kynareth, as craftsmen need to take resources from the natural world. With the boots, the path of faith was illuminated, and the pilgrim recovered the mace. [31]
Zenithar's Blessed Awl is a tool for weavers of thread, reed, and wood. Believers in the god of commerce Zenithar claim that "through the awl, all designs are made possible, threads hold strong, and work progresses easily."
Zenithar's Bleeding Whetstone is a lesser known artifact associated with god Zenithar . Little is known of the sickle or of the practices it was tied to, though it was said to have been associated with blood rites long before such rituals became linked with sinister or macabre connotations. According to Sanguine it was quickly forgotten.
Zenithar's Warning and Zenithar's Wiles are a set of gloves associated with the God of Commerce Zenithar . They were imbued with magical properties and effects such as reducing an adversary's ability and willingness to fight, and increasing people's disposition towards the wearer.
The planet Zenithar is one of the planets found in the skies of Mundus . Mara is Zenithar's satellite and is orbiting around it. As with all astral bodies seen in the skies above Nirn and their corresponding deities, the planet Zenithar is believed to be the plane of the god Zenithar as well as the god himself, as seen from the mortal plane. It is said to actually be a different plane of existence in its own right, infinite in size and mass, with its appearance as a sphere being only a visual phenomenon caused by mortal mental stress. According to some accounts, the mortal realms were formed by the Aedra out of Creatia .

Heir to the Seat of Sundered Kings, Talos (meaning Stormcrown in the language of the ancient Ehlnofey ), [1] also called King of Earth and Sky, Dragon of the North, the Dragon Prince, the Most High, and Lord of Shining Hosts, [2] is the most important hero-god of Mankind. In life, he was Emperor Tiber Septim , who conquered all of Tamriel and founded the Third Empire , ushering in the Third Era . Talos, or Stormcrown, is the name given to him at birth in Atmora . [1] Also called Ysmir , 'Dragon of the North', and General Talos. He is also referred to as 'the Grey Wind' and 'the Storm of Kyne' by the Greybeards . [3]
Amulets of Talos, traditionally crafted with Dragonbone and scales, [4] are used in worship of the God.
Tiber Septim died in 3E 38 , [5] and it is believed he ascended to godhood and became the ninth aspect of the Nine Divines , the major gods of Tamriel. These gods are alternatively referenced as the "eight and one" because of his distinct origins, the eight other Aedra arising from the interplay of Anu and Padomay before the creation of the Mundus or man. [6] The Divine Talos is the patron of questing adventurers, warriors, and generals, and was perhaps the most glorified god of human races, as Tiber Septim was a man and united much of the lands now comprising the Empire . Less prominent sects, such as the Talos Cult and Order of Talos , have also been devoted to the worship of Talos. Tokens commonly associated with the original Eight Divines were hybrids of Nordic and Aldmeri gods, often thought to be the same entities (e.g., Auri-El and Akatosh ). [7]
In the Fourth Era , worship of Talos was banned as a result of the White-Gold Concordat between the Aldmeri Dominion and the Empire, with the Dominion claiming that a man couldn't achieve apotheosis. [8]
The ban on Talos worship was initially only loosely enforced by the Empire, with people still having their shrines to Talos, until the Markarth Incident. In the chaos of the Great War, the Reachmen seized Markarth and declared independence, hoping to rejoin the Empire as an Imperial province. However, Ulfric Stormcloak led a Nord militia to crush this uprising, and for compensation demanded a repeal on the ban of Talos worship, which the Jarl of the Reach was forced to grant. This in turn brought the attention of the Aldmeri Dominion who demanded the right for the Thalmor to hunt and arrest those under the suspicion of Talos worship, or else the Empire would be perceived as violating the treaty. The issue of Talos worship continued to divide the Nords, and would ultimately lead to the Stormcloak Rebellion that erupted into the Skyrim Civil War , led by Ulfric Stormcloak.
The levitating Boots of the Apostle are attributed to the Emperor, though it remains veiled in mystery. [9] The Armor of Tiber Septim played an important role in helping to end the Oblivion Crisis in 3E 433 . [10]

Lorkhan , the Missing God, is the Creator-Trickster-Tester deity present in every Tamrielic mythic tradition. [3] He is known as the Spirit of Nirn, the god of all mortals. [1] His most popular name is the elven variant Lorkhan, or Doom Drum, [3] which sometimes appears as LKHAN [4] or LHKAN. [5] Names for versions or aspects of Lorkhan include Lorkhaj (the Moon Beast) in Elsweyr , [6] and Lorkh , the Spirit of Man, the Mortal Spirit, or the Sower of Flesh among the Reachmen . [7] He is recognized as Sep in Hammerfell , Sheor in High Rock , Shor in Skyrim , and Shezarr in Cyrodiil . [8] To the Altmer , he is also called the Corpse-God. [9] He is also known as the Void Ghost. [10] He convinced or contrived the Original Spirits ( et'Ada ) to bring about the creation of the Mundus , upsetting the status quo—much like his father Padomay or Sithis is usually credited for introducing instability, and hence possibility for creation, into the undivided universe (or the Beginning Place ). [3] [8] [11]
Lorkhan is sometimes associated with the number 9, [12] and has been referred to as the Missing Sibling or Missing Ninth to the Eight Divines . [13] [14] [UOL 2]
As with most other gods, accounts of Lorkhan and his various feats date back to the Dawn Era . [11] Owing to the non-linear nature of time in the Dawn, the War of Manifest Metaphors is said to have rendered stories of this era incapable of supporting anything resembling a singular "narrative", [15] rendering attempts at constructing one absurd. [16] Nonetheless, various people and cultures have their own views on Lorkhan's actions and motivations, with varying levels of consistent motifs. Every Tamrielic mythic tradition has an archetypal Missing God in its pantheon. This god is credited with convincing, tricking, or coercing the Original Spirits into creating the Mundus , the Mortal Plane.
After the world had been materialized, myths hold that Lorkhan was killed, mutilated, or at least separated from his divine center, known by various terms across cultures but most commonly dubbed the Heart of Lorkhan , in an event sometimes called the Convention . [11] [3] [8] This separation, which purportedly took place in Tamriel, [17] is variously considered a punishment, a betrayal, or voluntary sacrifice. [3] [18] [19] [20] Whatever remained of Lorkhan afterward was exiled to wander through creation, while the rest of the gods withdrew from the Mortal Realm. [3] [11] [21]
The result of these events, Nirn, has been described by some as a source of mental anguish to all living things, who know deep down that their souls came from somewhere else, and that something must come next. Many wish to return to the spirit realm , the original state of the universe; to them, Nirn is a prison keeping them sundered from the spirit realm, an illusion to escape, and Lorkhan is often the demon that hinders their way. Others think that Lorkhan created the world as the testing ground for transcendence; to them the spirit realm was already a prison, and that true escape is now finally possible. [1]
As the impetus for creating the Mundus, Lorkhan is at the center of the great ideological schism between the elven and human perspectives on the event, which is known as the Sundering to the elves or simply the Creation to other cultures, and thus opinions of him vary dramatically across the different religions of Tamriel. [3] Listed below are the more popular interpretations.
The most popular name of the Missing God is his Aldmeri moniker, "Lorkhan", meaning "Doom Drum", and literally translated as "Dark Chief" [22] [23] . For most Aldmeri cultures, particularly the Altmer, Lorkhan is a malevolent trickster, and the most unholy of all the higher powers. [8] Lorkhan is believed to have forever sundered their connection to the spirit plane . [1]
The most detailed account of these beliefs is found in the Altmeri creation Myth, "Heart of the World". Within the tale, Lorkhan is characterized as "a barely formed urge" more akin to the limitations of Sithis rather than a nature, unlike other spirits born from the ruminations of Anuiel and his soul, Auri-El . Eventually, Lorkhan infiltrates the various aspects of Anuiel to plant a new idea, one based wholly on limitation. He outlined a plan to create a soul for Aurbis, the Mundus, so the spirits may self-reflect like Anu once did, and form their own cosmos. Many spirits joined in, including Auriel, who agreed on the assurance that he would be king of this new realm. This offer turned out to be a trick, as the new world was hardly a thing of Anu at all - Mundus was the House of Sithis . As the spirits on the newly formed Nirn began to die off, many of the et'Ada vanished completely. Some, following Magnus 's example, fled to Aetherius and became the stars . Some, such as Y'ffre , fully merged with the world and transformed into the natural laws known as Earthbones , or Ehlnofey. Among those, a fourth group endured by marrying and making children with one another, living on through their descendants. [3] These Ehlnofey are known to the Altmer and Bosmer as Aedra , meaning Ancestors. [11]
Eventually, over many generations these children weakened into the Aldmer , whom Auriel continued to lead as they founded the great kingdoms of Altmora and Aldmeris, also known as Old Ehlnofey . Meanwhile, the weakest of the surviving souls were gathered up by Lorkhan, who named them Men and led their armies against Auriel. In the ensuing war, Lorkhan destroyed the kingdom of Old Ehlnofey, shattering that land into many. [3] [8] Eventually, the greatest knight of the Ehlnofey and champion of Auriel [24] :Part 1, Prologue, Trinimac , knocked Lorkhan down in front of his army and reached in "with more than hands" to remove his Heart. Lorkhan was undone, and his body was carried away by his vengeful followers. Despite Lorkhan's end, his Heart lived on, as it had become the Heart of the World and thus indestructible. Auriel then took the Heart and fastened it to an arrow, launching it into the sea where none may find it. [3]
Some less mythic accounts from the Altmer describe Lorkhan's end as a trial undergone within the Adamantine Tower by the Aedra. There, Lorkhan was judged guilty and Auriel slew him atop the Tower, removing his divine spark as punishment. Much like the "Heart of the World" account, the Heart proved to be indestructible, and was instead launched from the tower by an arrow from Auriel's Bow and landed in the Sea, where a volcano formed as a result. Afterward, the gods withdraw from the mortal realm, while Lorkhan remains exiled to wander Nirn forever. [25] [11]
Lorkhan is believed by some Bosmer to still exist under the Red Mountain , and that the rumblings of the volcano are a result of him eating food that upset his intestines. [26] Some Aldmeri Creation myths hold that Sheogorath is 'born' when Lorkhan's divine spark is removed. One crucial myth calls him the 'Sithis-shaped hole' of the world. [8]
In contrast to their fellow elves, Dunmeri culture, especially under Tribunal rule, always had a close relationship with the Missing God.
The Dunmeri Creation Myth, " Sithis ", characterizes Lorkhan as a cruel but ultimately benevolent god, who was created and sent by the titular primordial force to free the slaves of the false gods, Anuiel and the Aedra, as well as destroy their illusionary realms of everlasting imperfection that they used to make themselves immortal. Lorkhan does this via deceit, first creating his own dominion with what appears to be his own slaves, and then coming to Anuiel and the Aedra as a friend. Lorkhan told them that he was tired of his dominion, and wanted to create another. Magnus was the first to express his opinion on this, and eventually Nirn was created. [4] [TRL 1] However, according to the teachings of Boethiah, Lorkhan was betrayed by Magnus and the Aedra when he was not looking, and undone by them. [27]
Later Dunmeri legends claim that Boethiah 's creation of the Chimer and the subsequent Velothi Exodus were directly inspired by Lorkhan's ideas. Trinimac had tricked the Aldmer into believing tears were the best response to the Sundering, and took the Missing God's name in vain, calling his narratives into question. So one day, Boethiah came and defeated Trinimac, using Trinimac's own guise to expose his lies, and inspired his many new followers to head east, to Morrowind. [28] These followers, led by Veloth, became known as the Chimer, and Boethiah bade them to pledge themselves to the 'Frame-Maker', one of Lorkhan's many titles, for creating this world for them to cherish and love him in. [27]
Together, Boethiah and Mephala established the rules of the Psijic Endeavor , a process considered closely tied to Lorkhan, where the Chimer are charged with transcending the Aedra. [8] [UOL 3] Dunmer acknowledge Nirn to be a cruel place full of mental anguish, however they are among those who believe Lorkhan created a realm where true escape is now finally possible. [1] [UOL 3] [8] [10]
In later years, Lorkhan's Heart would become the source of Dagoth Ur and the Tribunal's divinity, with Lorkhan and his motivations becoming a pondered topic in the 36 Lessons of Vivec . The Scarab and the Serpent Constellation are Dunmeri symbols for Lorkhan. [27] [29] [10] The Black Scarab in particular is the symbol of House Redoran . [30] [31]
In Vivec's sermons, it is said that at the Altar of Padhome, there exists a secret door , one created by Lorkhan to show the mortals the secret at the Heart of the Beginning Place . [5] [32] [10] . This door is the promise of PSJJJJ, which is the flowering scheme of the Aurbis, and by following it one can fulfill Lorkhan's grand design for the Mundus, wherein it reaches the end of its usefulness and becomes a receptacle for something new. [33] [34] Additionally, Vivec's forbidden eighteenth sermon claims that Lorkhan has already vanished in the west, replaced by someone in the same manner that Tribunal replaced the Good Daedra in Morrowind. [35]
In his thirty-third sermon, Vivec's child, Lie Rock , met with the Void Ghost, an aspect of Lorkhan that dwells within the "Hidden Heaven", the Scaled Blanket made of not-stars , haggling with it for powers only meant for Ruling Kings. [10] Later the sermon describes a meeting between Nerevar and the Void Ghost, Nerevar compares himself to Lorkhan, asking which of them are out of place, to which Lorkhan replied both. While the sermon does not say what was spoken between the two masters, Vivec's numerology explains that the thirty-third sermon teaches the nature of Anticipations. [36] [10] In a similar vein, the "Sithis" creation myth compares Dagoth Ur to Anuiel and the Aedra, while the Nerevarine is likened to Lorkhan and advised to go unto the Sharmat Dagoth Ur as a friend, so that he may cast down the false gods as Lorkhan once did. [4]
Later in the Third Era, it is said Vivec shared further teachings with his followers after journeying to Cyrodiil to consult the Emperor on matters of the Tower. According to Vivec, the Missing God is Padhome's Firstborn, who wandered the Aurbis and wanted no name, yet was branded with "Lorkhan" by others. As time passed, Lorkhan watched the Aurbis shape itself and grew equally delighted and tired with each new shaping. As the gods and demons of the Aurbis erupted, the child of Padhome tried to leave it all behind for he wanted all of it and none of it all at once. It was then that he came to the border of the Aurbis. There, he saw the Tower, for a circle turned sideways is an "I". This was the first word of Lorkhan and he would never, ever forget it. [UOL 3]
According to the Sermons of Vivec, the Dwemer were merely aspirants to what the Velothi had. They regarded god planet LHKAN as one of the known worlds. [5] [UOL 2] A similar name, LKHAN, appeared in the phrase AE HERMA MORA ALTADOON PADHOME LKHAN AE AI, found across various esoteric texts seemingly unrelated to the Dwemer. [4] [37] [38]
To the Mythic Dawn, Lorkhan is the God of the Mundus, a Daedric Prince whose realm of Oblivion is Dawn's Beauty, misnamed Tamriel by deluded mortals. [39] [2] According to the teachings of Mankar Camoran , Tamriel is the realm of Change, and the Eight Divines are usurpers, betrayers and false gods posing as divinities for a Daedric Plane that has lost its Prince, whom they unsuccessfully attempted to erase from history. [2] Camoran claims that in Convention, the First Tower Dictate separated not only Lorkhan, but all mortals, his progeny, from their divine sparks, forcing mortals to see the Eight Aedra as the sole exit from the mortal prison. Further, in a secret message within his writings, he claims that the Eight Aedra woe every reader that has come to understand CHIM , the royalty Lorkhan taught mortals to seek. [39]
The Lunar Lorkhan Theory is an account of the Dawn proposed by the Temple Zero Society [nb 1] , intended to explain the origin of the Moons. [UOL 4] The theory claims that, where the Aedra contributed to the Great Construction by lending portions of their heavenly bodies , Lorkhan's was instead cracked asunder so that his divine spark might "impregnate Nirn with the measure of its existence and a reasonable amount of selfishness". What was left of his "flesh-divinity" became the Moons, a personification of what Artaeum calls the "Cloven Duality", the dichotomy that Lorkhan legends often rail against: ideas of good versus evil, being versus nothingness, and so on. The theory further asserts that the Moons were thus purposefully set in the night sky as Lorkhan's constant reminder to his mortal issue of their duty, and that all other ' Heart Stories ' are mythical degradations of the true origin of the moons. [15]
Known to the Khajiit as Lorkhaj , the Missing God is a divisive subject in Khajiiti theology. He is the youngest of Fadomai's children, born secretly in the Great Darkness after she was struck by Ahnurr , and thus forever haunted by the darkness that filled his Heart . Like other cultures, the Khajiit universally attribute the creation of the Mundus to Lorkhaj in their myths, however their attitude towards that event and his role in it has changed dramatically after the First Mane Rid-Thar-ri'Datta restructured the pantheon in what is known as the Riddle'Thar Epiphany . [6] [40]
In pre-ri'Datta theology, Lorkhaj was known to the Khajiit as the White Lion and the Favored Son of Fadomai. He was said to have been born in the Great Darkness and carried it with him as a burden, existing in conflict with himself from the moment of his birth. Lorkhaj was the first spirit to forge his path forward with purpose, and his courage inspired all those that he encountered, earning him the title of the Moon Prince as he united his siblings to create the World . [18] Afterwards, he suffered a great betrayal at the hands of the other spirits, which some songs attribute to the machinations of Merid-Nunda , and the Great Darkness overcame him, filling the hole where his heart had been. In his wisdom Lorkhaj, sought out his sister Azurah who tore the darkness from his chest before it could consume him, and she named it Namiira before casting it beyond the Void Gate. From this Dark Heart was born the Moon Beast, the first of the Dro-m'Athra and a shade of Lorkhaj that knows nothing but hunger, stalking the Lunar Lattice for any Khajiit that stray too far from the Path. The Moon Prince then expired in Azurah's arms, who burned his body upon a pyre and scattered the ashes across the Lunar Lattice . [19] [41] Thus, to the ancient Khajiit Lorkhaj represented the duality of the Khajiiti soul and the hardships that all Khajiit must overcome, and they honored his sacrifice by walking the Path with purpose and resisting the call of the Dark. [18]
Later Khajiiti theologians look less favorably upon Lorkhaj, disregarding his Moon Prince aspect entirely as a dangerous trick that would mislead the Khajiit into attempting to revive the Moon Beast's true spirit only to be swallowed by his eternal curse . [40] According to a post-ri'Datta creation myth, "Words of Clan-Mother Ahnissi", Fadomai birthed the last of her litter in the Great Darkness , having hidden there from her husband Ahnurr after he had beaten her. Thus, the Great Darkness filled the Heart of Lorkhaj and when he was born, the Great Darkness knew itself to be Namiira . Eventually, the spirit Nirni requested that Lorkhaj make her a place where she may birth many children, as Fadomai instructed her. Lorkhaj agreed, but because his heart was filled with darkness, he tricked his siblings so that they were forced into this new place with Nirni and trapped them within. Many of Fadomai's children escaped and became the stars, and many others died to make Nirni's path stable. The survivors stayed and punished Lorkhaj, tearing out his Heart and hiding it deep within Nirni. And though Nirni herself later forgave him, for now she could make children and had a place for them, the other spirits cursed "noisy Lorkhaj" to wander Nirni for many phases, forbidden by Azurah from hearing the First Secret while the other spirits learned how to cross the Lunar Lattice and escape. [6]
Both modern and ancient Khajiiti religion places the two moons, Jone and Jode, as separate entities that existed prior to Lorkhan's punishment at Convention, [6] and only believe that the third moon, the Dark Moon , is his corpse. In pre-ri'Datta tradition, they were stillborn twins, and it was their lanterns with which Azurah lit Lorkhaj's funeral pyre. [42] [43] [44] [18]
Known to the Ayleids (or specifically the star cults of Star-Queen Varalias), as Lorkh, the Missing God is considered the shadow of the Time God, Aka. Lorkh is said to have found the possibilities in nothing before Aka, and learned why his insanity is all that is and could be. By this lesson, the Adamantium Tower was set down, the laws of Convention enforced, to cleave a path to Numancia . [45]
Known to the Reachfolk as Lorkh , the Missing God is considered the Spirit of Man, the Mortal Spirit, or the Sower of Flesh.
According to Reach Myths, in darkness Lorkh came to understand, nothing does not exist. Where there is nothing there is possibility. And so he found a space in the Void where all that is could be. With this in mind he came to the Spirit Queen, Namira, to grant him a place in the infinite void to make a realm for wayward spirits, Nirn. Rather than a paradise, Lorkh created a hard and painful place—a realm that taught through suffering. [46] The early Reachmen fled to the mountains as with this creation Lorkh met his demise, and the world grew dark with shadows which sprung from the heart of Lorkh, who though greatly sundered still believed in the light of Man. [47] Reach Myth tells that Lorkh still wanders Nirn among mortals, appearing only in times of desperate need to aid the Reachfolk. [46]
The Reachmen believe that at the "end of all days", Hircine will fight alongside them, [48] and the Dark Heart of Lorkh will beat again, reawakened by feeding on the deaths of mortals, and its darkness will spread from its depths to consume everything, sparing only Namira 's faithful. [49]
It is thought the Briarheart ritual is a reflection by the immortal sacrifice of Lorkh. [46]
Known to the Nords as Shor , the Fox Totem [50] , the Missing God is considered the God of the Underworld who wielded his clever magic to create their afterlife of Sovngarde . [51] The Nords revere him for having fought off Aldmeri oppression many times in Atmora. [8] His widow is the warrior goddess Kyne , the purported creator of mankind and leader of the gods after Shor's demise. [8] [52]
Shor is said to have died at the hands of Elven Giants, who ripped out his heart at the end of the war that brought about the current world. [52] [53] The Nords believe this has happened before, as they consider the World itself to be a cycle in which the chief, Shor father of Shor, perishes every iteration of the world, spitting a helix of ghosts into the world below to make way for his son, Shor son of Shor, who leads mankind into war again. [UOL 5] In more modern Nordic understanding, this is the transition from Dead Gods to Twilight Gods that occurs over each new iteration of the world, marked by the coming of the Last War in which Shor and his host descend from Sovngarde to fight again as they have at the beginning of time. [52] [54] [55] [UOL 6] Some myths consider All-Maker the father of Shor. [UOL 7]
Shor is believed to wander in times of need as a ghost, sung into the world by the chant of Tongues. He once appeared to fight Alduin as he did in the beginning of time, and save the Nords from trickery of Orkey . [53] [8] Another mythic account describes his appearance at the Battle of Red Mountain, where he led the Nords against the armies of the First Council , with Ysmir Wulfharth and Dagoth Ur serving as his generals. [53] In Cyrodilic legends, he is tied to mortal incarnations such as "the Shezarrine ", who has also been called "Shor-Who-Lives". [56] Pelinal Whitestrake is one alleged Shezarrine, and Nords who saw him and his white hair proclaimed that Shor had returned, though Pelinal himself called this profanity. [57]
Known to the Bretons as Sheor , the Missing God is considered the "Bad Man", a demonized variation of Shor that emerged in the dark years after Saarthal , as a result of association with Nord conquest. [8] [58] He is said to be the source of all strife and is considered a god of bad crop failure. [8] Seemingly pre-Saarthal early manmeri depictions of Mara depict Sheor (Lorkhan, then) as her lover. [59]
Known to the Cyrodiils (particularly the subculture of the Nibenese), as Shezarr , the Missing God is considered "the spirit behind all human undertaking" and the God of Man. [13] [8] He is deeply revered as an early hero of the Nedes, and is considered to be synonymous with "Freedom" in the myths of the Alessian Rebellion . [60]
According to the Cyrodilic Creation Myth, "Shezarr's Song", Shezarr came to the Aedra with a new idea: to become mothers and fathers, and make great sacrifices with no guarantee of success. Shezarr spoke beautifully to them, and moved them beyond mystery and tears. In some legends, Kynareth is first to agree to Shezarr's proposal, and provided the space for this creation in the void. [8] Thus the Aedra gave free birth to the world, the beasts, and the beings, making these things from parts of themselves. This free birth was very painful, and afterwards the Aedra were no longer young, and strong, and powerful, as they had been from the beginning of days.
The Daedra mocked Shezarr and the other Aedra, and decided to make their own worlds within themselves where they wouldn't have to sacrifice control, though they came to envy the Aedra's creation. Some Aedra were disappointed, bitter, and angry with Shezarr, and with all creation, for they felt Shezarr had lied and tricked them. These Aldmeri gods, led by Auri-El , resolved to teach the elves to suffer with dignity, to chastise themselves for their folly, and to seek vengeance on Shezarr and his allies for reducing them to their enfeebled selves. But the gods of men and beast folk, led by Akatosh, were pleased with creation despite their great sacrifices, and resolved to teach the mortal races to live well, to cherish beauty and honor, and to love one another as the gods love them. [3]
Rather than claim his Aldmeri counterpart is a responsible, some second era Cyrodilic bard songs claim Akatosh directly slew Shezarr (Lorkhan in the song), launching his laughing immortal heart across Tamriel. The Heart's Blood is said in these tales to have fallen into an Ayleid well and fused with the magicka therein, solidifying into a crystal that the Ayleids cut and polished into the Red Diamond, Chim-El Adabal . [61]
Despite Shezarr's demise, he continued to wander Tamriel, aiding the tribes of man at various points as a warrior or a wise teacher before vanishing again to help mankind elsewhere. [13] [56] Some believe Shezarr did not do this directly, but through mortal incarnates known as " the Shezarrine ", or "Shor-Who-Lives". [62] [63] [56] Pelinal Whitestrake was once said to be the alleged Shezarrine, although this was best left unsaid in Pelinal's presence as Plontinu, a soldier who fought alongside Pelinal, was said to have been smothered by moths in the night after voicing the claim. [63] Some claim it was Pelinal or Shezarr, rather than Akatosh, that met Alessia at her deathbed, making her the first stone of the Amulet of Kings and forging the Covenant . [64] [UOL 8]
It is said that Kynareth made the first rain at Shezarr's sundering, a phenomenon that did not exist before the removal of his Heart. [8] [60]
The Missing God is known to the Redguards as Sep, the Second Serpent created from the detritus of the First Serpent Satakal 's dead skin.
According to the Yokudan Creation Myth, "Satakal the Worldskin", Satakal was born from the duality of Satak, the Snake who came Before, and Akel, the Hungry Stomach. Because there was nothing outside of Satak, it could only think about what it was, and so it devoured itself until its hunger caused it to bite down on its own heart and die. The hunger, though, refused to stop even in death, and so the First Serpent shed its dead skin to begin anew as Satakal. Once the other spirits realized this pattern , the strongest of them learned to survive this cycle by striding between the worldskins at strange angles, in a process they called the Walkabout. One of the spirits, Ruptga , was so tall he could place the stars in the sky to help weaker spirits to find their way to the Far Shores , and sired many children across the cycles until he became known as Tall Papa.
Eventually, there were so many spirits that Tall Papa had to make himself a helper from the detritus of dead skins, and this was Sep, the Second Serpent. However, Sep carried much of the Hungry Stomach within him, multiple hungers from multiple dead skins, and would often devour the spirits he was supposed to help. When he did, Ruptga would reach in and pull them back out. After some time, Sep grew tired of helping Ruptga, and so he gathered all the dead worldskins into a ball. He tricked the other spirits, promising them this was how you reached the new world, by making one out of the old, and many joined him thinking this was an easier way of living, without jumping from place to place all the time. However, soon the spirits on the skin-ball began to die, as it was far removed from the real world of Satakal, and it was too far now for them to jump into the Far Shores, and Ruptga said they had to live on through their children and search for new ways to follow the stars. Sep, however, needed more punishment, and so Ruptga struck him with a big stick , and his hunger fell out of his mouth and was the only thing left of the Second Serpent. While the rest of the world strived back to godhood, Sep could only wander Nirn, trapped in his own dead skin, or swim about in the sky as the Serpent Constellation, a hungry void that jealously tries to eat the stars. [3]
Allegedly known to the Argonians of the Nisswo and Adzi-Kostleel tribes is a Time God, Atakota, and his nameless shadow. [65]
According to the Adzi-Kostleel Creation Myth, "Children of the Root", Atak and Kota become one, Atakota is born and says "Maybe", it sheds its skin. The skin follows it around as a Shadow. As Atakota coiled and devours worlds , each a scale on its skin, things had time to begin and end. The Shadow wished to eat these things, but its belly was full of growing Roots . The Shadow soon spat out the Roots, and regarded them as its own, and so taught them secrets before falling into slumber.
