Note: The following references are considered to be unofficial sources . They are included to round off this article and may not be authoritative or conclusive.
Alkosh • Baan Dar • Jode • Jone • Khenarthi • Mara • Riddle'Thar • S'rendarr
Molag Bal (Stone-Fire, [1] or literally the "Fire Stone") [2] [nb 1] is the Daedric Prince whose sphere is the domination and enslavement of mortals. [3] His ultimate desire is to harvest the souls of all mortals, bringing them within his sway by spreading seeds of strife and discord in the mortal realms. [4] [5] To this end, he obsessively collects soul gems, [6] [7] and has even dragged pieces of Nirn into his realm to satisfy his insatiable desire for conquest. [8] The more souls he collects, the more he wants. [9]
Molag Bal values patience and cunning. He often deceives those he deals with, and has no trouble waiting exceedingly long periods of time for his plans to come to fruition. [10] [11] [12] He also takes great pleasure in the suffering of mortals, and often has them tortured for his amusement. [13] Those who fall into his clutches are forced to labor ceaselessly for his benefit. [14] The Prince has a penchant for necromancy, [15] [16] often employing the use of the risen dead and forcing his followers to serve beyond the grave. [17] He is capable of preserving live mortals far beyond their normal lifespan to prolong their suffering. [18]
Molag Bal is acknowledged as the father of several children, though it is unclear what those relationships mean to him. [19] He has a son, Ozozzachar , [20] who is a Daedric Titan . [21] He also has a daughter named Molag Grunda , a Winged Twilight who took the lowly Frost Atronach Nomeg Gwai as her consort. Molag Bal did not approve of the relationship and had them both banished to Oblivion so he could punish them for eternity. [22] A persistent legend about Haymon Camoran claims he is the progeny of Molag Bal and a Breton woman. [23]
Molag Bal's enemies are Ebonarm , [24] Boethiah , [25] Meridia , [26] Lamae Bal [27] and Arkay . He was at one point allied with Azura . [24] His summoning day is during Chil'a , on the 20th of Evening Star. [28] He appears before his cultists in the guise of a mortal when invoked on other dates. [29]
It is rumored among the kyn who serve him that his second-favorite type of weapon after the mace is the axe, as it is the tool of the headsman. However, nobody is brave enough to ask him about it. [30] He purportedly has terrible hygiene and smells of rotting flesh. [31]
Mortals have often used descriptors and alternate names to refer to the Daedric Prince in conversation. Molag Bal is unique in that he is known by an exceedingly large amount of nicknames, some used by specific groups for different reasons. He is most commonly referred to as the God of Schemes, [32] the Harvester of Souls, [33] [34] [35] the Lord of Domination, [36] the Prince of Domination [37] and the Lord of Brutality. [38] [39] These terms are comparatively neutral, and can be used by anyone to describe him, from normal citizens to his greatest enemies and his most devout worshippers. These titles are descriptive, and utilize aspects of his sphere of influence to identify him as the subject of conversation. The Dark Lord is another neutral name, used by fearful slaves in hushed whispers, and uttered by the Prince's enemies in defiance. [40] [41] [42] Molag Bal's minions use the name in reverence. [43] [44]
Several other nicknames which the mortals of Tamriel have identified Molag Bal with include the Harvester, [45] the Lord of Corruption, [46] :293 the Corrupter, the Tormentor of Men, the Corrupter of Souls, [47] the Lord of Troubles, [48] [49] the Prince of Schemes, [50] [51] the God of Brutality, [52] the Dominator, [53] the Sower of Strife [54] the Dark Lord of Torture [55] and the Scheming Lord of Coldharbour. [56] These nicknames share a common theme in that they describe Molag Bal's sphere of influence. These titles also draw from his most easily recognizable behaviors. Molag Bal is known to harvest souls, torment mortals for fun and corrupt the minds of good people with promises of power. [57] He hatches elaborate schemes to spread his influence across Tamriel. The Prince of Murder is a more vague title that has been affixed to the Prince by the servants of his enemy, Meridia. [58] The title does not frame murder as an aspect of his sphere in the way that it falls under Mephala's divine portfolio; rather, it illustrates the idea that Molag Bal is an individual of an unpleasant nature. In 2E 582 , an ally of the Army of Meridia referred to Molag Bal as the Dark One. [42]
The King of Rape [59] [60] is a title that is used in the Living God Vivec 's sermons. It alludes to his desire to "ruin the Dunmer gene pool", as outlined in The House of Troubles . [59] The Tribunal Temple has also referred to him as the Father of Monsters. [61] The Elder Spirit of Domination and Supreme Law is a moniker derived from the epistles of Amun-dro , an ancient Khajiit priest who committed the names of the old Khajiit deities to paper. [62] Carlovac Townway referred to Molag Bal as the Prince of Rage in his popular historical fiction novel, 2920, The Last Year of the First Era . [63] Sigillah Parate identified Molag Bal as the Schemer Princess in her work, Invocation of Azura . [29]
Molag the Slave-Lord [65] is a one-off nickname used by Phrastus of Elinhir in his lecture on the nature of Molag Bal's plane of Oblivion, Coldharbour. The name is in reference to Molag Bal's voracious desire to amass unfathomable amounts of souls, and his tendency to enslave those whose souls he comes into possession of. The Schemer, [66] the Prince of Pain [67] and the Captain of Corruption are monikers written in popular bardic performances from the Interregnum. The Plane Meld was a song that described the impending doom from the then-ongoing Planemeld. It likens Molag Bal to some sort of demented farmer, tilling the land of Tamriel and sowing corruption as far as he can reach. [68]
The Dark Father, [69] [70] the Father of Vampires, [71] [72] Lord of the Undead, [73] and the Father of Undeath [9] are nicknames derived from Molag Bal's association with vampires and the undead . Regardless of whether or not they revere him, vampires who recognize Molag Bal as the progenitor of the first vampire may refer to him as the Dark Father. Scholars and laymen who are familiar with Molag Bal's association with vampires or his propensity for raising the dead may refer to him as either of the latter. [69] The Defiler [27] is a title of note among the scions of Lamae Bal , the first vampire. The vampires of Lamae's coven are encouraged to despise Molag Bal, and they acknowledge him as the one responsible for their matron's state of undeath. [74] The scions of Lamae have also referred to Molag Bal as the Father of Torment and the King of Corruption [74] [75] in light of the notion that Molag Bal takes great delight in the suffering of mortals, and actively seeks to corrupt anything he believes will further his goals or spread strife throughout Nirn.
The Lord of Lies, [76] [67] the Master of Domination [77] and the Lord of Darkness [78] are names that have been uttered by the Prince's adherents. The Dark Master [76] [79] and the Dread Lord [80] are titles which have been explicitly used by Molag Bal's cultists and Daedric minions in the context of their service. The Daedra who serve Molag Bal refer to him as "the Dread Lord", "the Dark Lord" and their "Brutal Lord" [81] in their missives. Servants both mortal and otherwise have identified their god with many variations on the words "lord" and "master", denoting the Prince's position of power above the denizens of his Plane. [76] [82] An example of such a name is the Lord of Schemes. [83] [84] The Lord of Coldharbour is a simple title that refers to his dominion over Coldharbour. [85]
Alongside Azura and Nocturnal , the Daedra worshiping Corelanya Clan revered Molag Bal as one of their primary Daedric patrons. The Corelanyan High Elves prayed to him for strength when the need to defend their livelihood arose. [86] Molag Bal fell out of favor around 1E 2633 , after the Three Queens rose as the Corelanyan's guardian triumvirate. [87]
Molag Bal was known as Mola Gbal to the Ayleids , [88] and was the patron deity of Abagarlas . [89] [90]
The Bretons of the Iliac Bay share stories about the "Terrible Torchbug of Molag Bal" the night before Waking Day . The stories vary, but the one consistent part of every variation of the tale involves a fearsome, savage entity being followed by blue torchbugs. [91] Some people in the Iliac Bay believe that when Molag Bal is irate, his anger can be seen in the sky as a bright red star. [92]
Molag Bal is a Daedric power of much importance in Morrowind , where he is always the archenemy of Boethiah , the Prince of Plots. He is seen as a primary obstacle to the Dunmer people (and their Chimer predecessors). In the legends, Molag Bal always tries to upset the bloodlines of Houses or otherwise ruin Dunmeri "purity". [93] He is said to be the parent (along with Vivec ) of a population of supermonsters that terrorized the Molag Amur region of Vvardenfell . [94] [95]
He is known to the Dunmer as the third corner of the House of Troubles , and one of the Bad Daedra. [96] [97] Dunmeri mythos cites Bal Ur as the "birthplace of Molag Bal", [98] where the Doom Drum tricked him into becoming mortal. [99] Bal Ur is a place of great importance for Vvardenfell's vampire clans, particularly the Aundae , who believe the ruin is important to their vampire heritage. [48] In the days of old Resdayn, Molag Bal was not worshipped so much as he was placated. [100]
Molag Bal plays a pivotal role in the narrative of Vivec's famous sermons. The Prince and the Warrior-Poet parleyed in the wilds of Molag Amur, [60] an event which served as the catalyst for the creation of a variety of monsters which the tribune would gradually slay throughout the rest of the sermons. During their time together, Molag Bal taught the God-Poet the secret syllable of royalty, CHIM, which, in his lessons, Vivec described as essential to becoming a Ruling King of the world. Their meeting ended in treachery as great monsters arose from the earth in the wake of their union, and Vivec vanquished the Daedric Prince in kind. [94] The Shrine of Inevitable Secrets, a temple devoted to Hermaeus Mora in Morrowind, was dragged beneath the waves by dreugh. Some legends attribute the shrine's disappearance to Vivec and Molag Bal's fury upon learning that the Lord of Secrets had spied on them, removing his shrine from the world and disallowing more to be built as punishment. [101]
Molag Bal purportedly has connections to the dreugh . According to legend, the dreugh used to be far more intelligent and civilized than they are today. The dreugh were said to worship a creature known as the Ruddy Man . Not much is known about this entity, but it is believed to be a manifestation of Molag Bal. [102] Dunmeri folklore alleges that Molag Bal was the chieftain of the dreugh in a time when they ruled the world. During that time, Molag Bal took on a form that was spiny, armored, and built for life beneath the waves. The Ruddy Man from the teachings of the Tribunal Temple is said to resemble this iteration of Molag Bal, and was supposedly a product of Vivec and Molag Bal's union. [103]
Brutal master, lord of troubles Giver of strength to slay and strive
Molag Bal is venerated by some Reachman clans. The people of the Reach recognize the Lord of Lies as a schemer who enjoys his tricks. Known as the Father of Torment, the Defiler and He Who Schemes in the Shadows, Molag Bal is a spirit that represents hardship. [104] He's somewhat of a testing god, acknowledged as a spirit who brings trouble, and in doing so, he teaches the people of the Reach to endure. [105]
The Reachfolk understand that hardship is inevitable, but instead of bemoaning their sorrows, they embrace the pain as wisdom. [104] They look up to those who work hard, [106] and believe those that are plagued by troubled times are strengthened by their experiences. Molag Bal grants nothing but unexpected pain. Those of the Reach who venerate Molag Bal appreciate the lessons he teaches, growing resilient in his shadow. [104]
The Cinder-Heart Clan reveres the Defiler, [107] and their practices reflect the extreme horrors of Molag Bal's sphere. They are known for their cruel treatment of captives, whom they burn alive. Their briarheart ritual is augmented with the intense pain of opening a subject's chest cavity and filling it with hot coals. [108]
Reachmen are sometimes referred to as the Witchmen of High Rock , a name that stems from their use of dark "Reach-magic". [109] Although Hircine is believed to be the source of the dark nature magic used by Reach hedge wizards, [110] Molag Bal can grant similar power. The name "gravesinger" is worn by some Reachman necromancers who broker deals with Bal [111] in exchange for the power to command the loathsome dead. [112] These gravesingers can command corpses by planting the seeds of foul vines in their victims' bodies. Molag Bal's "gifts" may leave a gravesinger's body in a state of decay. [113]
Some ancient Khajiit tribes knew him as Molagh, one of the twelve Demon Kings and the Elder Spirit of Domination and Supreme Law. In some forms of Khajiiti mythos, Molagh has a wife who freed Merrunz to weaponize his destructive nature. Molagh was the first to attempt an assault on the Lunar Lattice with intent alongside Merrunz and Merid-Nunda. Boethra fought him to a standstill before the Lattice, and Azurah brought him down. [62] A pre-ri'Datta text known as The Bladesongs of Boethra describes the battle at the Lattice in greater detail, alleging that Molagh taunted Boethra with the severed head of Lorkhaj to lure her away from guarding the Lunar Lattice, allowing Dagon and Merid-Nunda to break through the moonlight barrier. [114] Amun-dro's writings say that he is to be faced along the Path and overcome by the Will Against Rule, a concept associated with Boethra. Molag Bal is among those the Epistles of Amun-dro refer to as the Adversarial Spirits. [62]
Little is known about Maormer religion, but Keel Cutters, Maormer slave traders, were known to invoke the name of Molag Bal. [115]
Daedra worship was not prohibited by law in Cyrodiil, but public opinion and the religious establishment were so staunchly opposed to the notion, that many who worshipped Daedra did so in secret. [116] Molag Bal has a shrine in Cyrodiil's Great Forest , [117] and is listed as one of the 16 Acceptable Blasphemes in the first edition of the Pocket Guide to the Empire. [118]
One legend claims that Molag Bal created the first vampire when he raped a Nedic virgin named Lamae Beolfag , who in turn slaughtered a group of nomads. [119] He has also made pacts with mortals and bestowed the gift of vampirism upon them. However, they must be powerful in their own right before they can earn his trust; he only grants such gifts to his more impressive followers. [120] One such example is Lord Harkon and his family, who are implied to be the original Volkihar Clan of vampires, having been turned by the Daedric Prince himself. [121] Female followers of Molag Bal are traditionally offered to him on his summoning day. Being selected as an offering is an honor; selectees are expected to go through with the ritual. Few survive, and those who do emerge as pure-blooded vampires. [122] Molag Bal is also rumored to have bestowed upon ancient Vampire Lords several powerful artifacts that amplify vampiric powers. [123] Among these treasures is the Amulet of Bats , which was found in the possession of Lord Falgravn in 2E 582 . [124] Molag Bal's gifts are never given freely—those who seek to ask him for a favor must always pay a price, and it is usually paid in blood. [125]
One legend from Tribunal Temple tradition holds that Molag Bal spawned the first vampire upon the corpse of a defeated enemy. There are several different variations of the story, all of which identify this foe as a different entity: a Daedra Lord , a Temple Saint , and a powerful beast are among the things these stories claimed the foe to be. This version of the vampire creation myth is unique to Morrowind. [61]
Cults that worship Molag Bal often perform live, unwilling sacrifices to please their god. [44] A core belief among his cults is that the weak must be punished by the strong, a tenet that Molag Bal himself touts. [25] This belief is held highly by the Dremora who serve the Lord of Lies. [126] The aforementioned cults use dark magic and necromancy as a tool, profaning the dead and using them to bring Molag Bal further toward his ultimate goal. [16] [127] Cunning, deception and subterfuge are just as valued by the Prince as brutality is. Thus, bribery, blackmail and extortion are common tools of the Stonefire Cult . [29] [128] Some groups, such as the Fen Witches of Hjaalmarch , engage in practices such as child abduction to bolster their ranks. [129] Those unaffiliated with a cult who wish to contact the Prince may offer a lion 's pelt at a shrine dedicated to Molag Bal. This practice has been observed in Cyrodiil . [117]
The Order of the Black Worm served Molag Bal during the Planemeld. [130] The Order was a prime example of a group that wielded necromancy in Molag Bal's name. In addition to raising an undead army in the wake of the Planemeld, the cult participated in the Planemeld in force, carrying out many schemes in his name. [131] [132] Additionally, they sacrificed thousands of souls to the Daedric Prince, [112] and were responsible for constructing and activating the dolmens where Dark Anchors were to be dropped. [133] [43]
Molag Bal will bless his followers with undeath under certain circumstances. During the Planemeld, the Order of the Black Worm allied with the Stonetalon , Rageclaw and Boneshaper Reach clans. [134] In exchange for releasing the ancient giant Sinmur , the Order promised to bless the clans with "the touch of their dark lord". [135] The Order channeled the touch of Molag Bal throughout Vernim Woods , transforming the Reachmen into undead monstrosities known as the Graveborn . [136] The leader of the Reachfolk clans who invaded the Rift , Stral Blackthroat , underwent the transformation himself within the nearby ruins, gaining immense power. [137] Molag Bal has also rewarded his followers with immortality through vampirism in return for sacrificing innocents in his name. [121] Those who offer themselves willingly to Molag Bal from beyond the grave may have the pleasure of being transformed into an undead abomination, be it a flaming bone colossus or some other monstrous creature. [138] [139] [140]
The Lord of Lies is the center of a longstanding philosophical debate known as the Molag Bal Paradox. Revolving around the notion that Molag Bal consistently defies the will of others (often suppressing their will by imposing his own upon them), the paradox poses the following question: "If everyone would express the belief that [Molag Bal] exists, would he therefore cease to do so because his nature demands he oppose the wishes of others?" Assuming that Molag Bal is subject to the metaphysical rules of the Aurbis, philosophers have argued whether or not the Prince "truly" embodies the concept of domination. [141] The Molag Bal Paradox is considered a classic puzzle, and scholars have engaged with it since at least the Second Era. [142]
The ancient Daedric temple-city of Mor Naril , built sometime in early history on the island of Solstice , was dedicated to Molag Bal. His servants built it to harvest souls departing the mortal plane. [143] The temple predates the Stone-Nest Argonians' arrival to the island, who were called over across the sea to settle on Solstice before the event known as Duskfall . [UOL 1] [144] [145] These Argonians considered the Daedra on the island their enemies, and constructed stone guardians known as Voskrona to wage war on them. [146] Ancient Stone-Nest figurines recovered on Solstice depict the tribe's warriors doing battle against Molag Bal. [147]
Kinlady Iniel Corelanya learned necromantic magics from Mor Naril during her ill-fated campaign to conquer Solstice in 1E 723 . She later called upon Molag Bal during the Corelanyans' war against the Yokudans in 1E 853 . [86] Her necromantic exploits caused a schism in her clan betweeen those who rejected the Daedra and those unafraid to petition them for aid. The Corelanyans eventually came to reside on Solstice, coexisting peacefully with the native Argonians after several centuries of conflict. The Peace of Xor-Hist ushered in an era of calm and prosperity that lasted until 1E 2631 , when the tyrant Kinlord Istamil Corelanya rose to power. He smothered dissent with impunity, and was largely unpopular, inciting a civil war. Desperate to maintain power, Istamil sought Molag Bal's assistance in silencing the opposition at the temple of Mor Naril. The Lord of Brutality bestowed legions of Daedra and great necromantic power unto Kinlord Istamil, who used these gifts to slaughter his enemies wholesale. Molag Bal's power ran unchecked until Istamil's cousin Vinutilmo rallied beneath the Three Queens, Meridia, Azura, and Nocturnal, to push back against the kinlord and his Daedric patron. Istamil was slain, and Mor Naril was ultimately sealed. [87]
Sometime in the First Era , Molag Bal made a pact with King Styriche of Verkarth , a powerful vampire and the leader of the Gray Host . [148] The terms of their agreement eventually brought thirteen of the Gray Host's exarchs into Coldharbour, where they were tortured for eternity by the Daedric Prince. [148] Rada al-Saran , the last known surviving leader of the Gray Host, sought to free his kin from Coldharbour in 2E 582 with the help of the Icereach Coven . [149] At some point, a Molag Bal-worshipping Ayleid clan that lived in Greenshade ceased making offerings to the Prince at Moonhenge . Their city was consumed in blue fire and transported to Coldharbour. [150]
In 1E 2260 , the All Flags Navy sailed to Thras and defeated the Sload . The Sloads' Coral Tower was destroyed during the siege, causing it to plunge into the sea. [151] The Maelstrom of Bal [152] opened up in the wake of the tower's fall, and a large chunk of the fleet was pulled beneath the Abecean Sea and into Coldharbour. Molag Bal appeared before the wreckage and struck a deal with the leader of the fallen fleet, giving the admiral complete control over his sailors with a relic known as the Crown of Bones. [151] [153] [154]
Molag Bal was responsible for the destruction of the Bosmeri settlement of Gil-Var-Delle at the end of the First Era , when he was summoned by King Dro'Zel of Senchal . [155] Molag Bal consumed the souls of all mortals present that day, leaving nothing more than the charred remains of the town's once mighty graht-oak . [156] His corrupting influence lingered into the second era. [51] [157] The disaster at Gil-Var-Delle was the catalyst that sparked the conception of the Coldharbour Compact . Molag Bal was present at the meeting where Sotha Sil sealed his famed bargain with the powers of Oblivion, and agreed to the terms the Living God set. [66]
Molag Bal is rumored to be responsible for the creation of the first soul gems . [UOL 2] This theory appears to have some grain of truth to it: Mannimarco and his cohorts, believing the Harvester held the secret to trapping souls, stole their first black soul gems from Coldharbour some time after the King of Worms' expulsion from Artaeum . [158] Natural black soul gem deposits exist within Coldharbour, and greater Daedra have been recorded setting up mining operations atop them. [159]
He is credited with the creation of two species of Daedra, both involved in his experiments in a facility known as the Vile Laboratory . [160] The Xivkyn [7] and the Daedric Titan are the products of his fascination with vestigial hybridization and the Dragons ' dominion over mortalkind respectively. Both of these species were released upon the people of Tamriel for the first time during Molag Bal's invasion of Nirn. [82]
In the 582nd year of the Second Era, Molag Bal attempted to merge Nirn with his plane of Coldharbour in an event known as the Planemeld . However, this campaign was halted by the Vestige . [161] A last-ditch effort to claim the Mundus for his own occurred not long after, while his forces occupied the Imperial City . [162] [163] [UOL 3] Led by the King of Worms, the Order of the Black Worm and an offshoot of Legion traitors dubbed Legion Zero served beneath Molag Bal's Daedric minions to invade and maintain control over the city. [164] Molag Bal's minions had converted the centrata, the central hub of the City's sewers beneath the White-Gold Tower , into a terminal for portals to Coldharbour. [162] Molag Bal sought to locate and destroy the Sublime Brazier so the Dragonfires could never be lit again. [165] [166] This plot, along with a ritual known as the Planemeld Obverse which involved an Elder Scroll , were also foiled. [163] [167]
Molag Bal also arranged to have biological weapons produced in the Imperial Prison . The City's citizens were to be brought to the prison and delivered to the prison's flesh sculptor , a powerful necromancer who held mastery over flesh. The sculptor would turn mortal skin and organs into carriers for disease and pollution, creating flesh devices that would spew noxious clouds to contaminate the earth around them. Molag Bal's generals planned to deploy a thousand of these siege engines throughout the City, ending the Alliance War and turning the tide in Molag Bal's favor. [168] [169]
Sometime prior to House Gardner 's fall, Princess Violetta of House Gardner used Birdsong Tower as her garden-spot. It was a pleasant place just west of Wayrest , where flowers bloomed and birds sang. Molag Bal was offended by the tower's joyous environment, and sent a plague of undeath to profane it. Birdsong Tower became known as Soulshriven Tower . [170] A hero curbed the tower's undead infestation in 2E 582 . [171] Molag Bal was also responsible for cursing the town of Westtry at some point between 2E 220 and 2E 582 . The town was invaded by Nord raiders, and a priest of Stendarr sought aid from his god. He was answered by Molag Bal instead, and begged the Prince to save Westtry. Molag Bal subsequently cursed Westtry with undeath. [172] The curse was lifted by the Vestige in 2E 582 . [173] That same year, Abagandra—the Lightless Remnant of the Doomcrag —was given to Molag Bal and transported to his realm to prevent it from devastating Rivenspire with a plague of vampirism. [174]
During Mannimarco's service to Molag Bal, the King of Worms schemed to assume Molag Bal's place as the God of Brutality. Using the Amulet of Kings , the most powerful soul gem the world has ever known, Mannimarco intended to trap Molag Bal's essence and use it to ascend to godhood. [175] This plot was foiled by the Vestige at Sancre Tor prior to the end of the Planemeld, and the God of Schemes dragged Mannimarco's soul to Coldharbour to administer punishment. [176]
Some time after Molag Bal's defeat, the Worm Cult began sacrificing souls to the Lord of Brutality in earnest with the help of magical engines known as soul reapers, seeking to curry the Daedric Prince's favor. [177] To this end, the cult slew Guildmaster Merric at-Aswala of the Fighters Guild and kidnapped Vanus Galerion . [178] [179] The cult gathered on the eastern half of the island of Solstice, erecting a barrier of soulstuff to bisect the island so they could work in secret. On their half of the wall, the cult prepared a "reverse Planemeld": while the original Planemeld sought to drag Nirn into Coldharbour, this new ritual sought to bring Coldharbour to the mortal realm. [180] This plan was set in motion with the ultimate goal of returning the wraithforge , a device capable of binding and deconstructing souls into immense energy, to Mor Naril. [181] A newly resurrected Mannimarco, inhabiting his nephew Wormblood 's body, [182] sought to use the device to restore his original body and continue his pursuit of godhood, [183] but was thwarted by the Stirk Fellowship . [184] To power the wraithforge for this task, the Worm Cult constructed devices known as Soul Flayers, which not only emitted necrotic energy that killed anything nearby, but harvested the souls of any mortal killed within said radius, sending them directly to the wraithforge. [185] [186] These devices were constructed in Molag Bal's image. [187]
Sometime around 3E 405 , in exchange for power, Molag Bal tasked the agent of the Blades with eliminating a heretic mage who invoked his name and drew from his power without a contract. [188] In 3E 427 , Molag Bal asked the Nerevarine to banish a lazy Daedroth minion named Menta Na to the Outer Realms, where he would be punished for not wreaking havoc as he was created for. [189] [190] During the Oblivion Crisis , Molag Bal spread corruption by having the Hero of Kvatch incite a local pacifist to murder. [117]
In 4E 201 , Molag Bal's presence haunted a home within the city of Markarth , and attracted the attention of a Priest of Stendarr who came to purify the residence. The Last Dragonborn offered to assist, and when the pair entered the home, Molag Bal trapped them inside. He forced the Dragonborn and the priest to fight to the death, and the Dragonborn prevailed, proving themselves the stronger of the two. With the Dragonborn having passed his "test", the Prince gave them a task: he offered them the Mace of Molag Bal as an incentive for capturing the soul of a priest of Boethiah who had been desecrating Molag Bal's shrine. The hero complied with the request. [25]
Molag Bal has been depicted several different ways by the sculptors who construct shrines dedicated to him. The only truly consistent trait across all portrayals is the presence of a tail, horns and/or frills, and a muscular frame with bestial legs. Some artists interpret him as a bipedal creature with the head of a reptile, long claws, three digits on each hand, and digitigrade legs on paws shaped like that of a lion [191] or a large reptile. [192] These depictions possess aquatic frills, and occasionally horns. [191] Molag Bal once manifested before an agent of the Blades as a green-skinned humanoid with red eyes, elven ears, the legs of a goat, a long, thin tail, a goat-like face, and curved black horns. [193] Acclaimed author Carlovac Townway described the destruction of Gilverdale in 2920, The Last Year of the First Era: by his account, bright red mist poured over the town in a wave, engulfing building after building in its wake, with Molag Bal arriving as a grinning, red-skinned giant. [194]
His most iconic appearance is depicted stylistically on his eponymous Mace: a face with ridged horns, aquatic frills, and sharp fangs. During the Planemeld, Molag Bal appeared in the form that inspired the Mace's rendering of his face. Molag Bal possesses a broad, sinewy, muscular frame carried by digitigrade legs with hips crowned by jagged protrusions. A row of spikes ran from the top of his head down the length of his back, and a bony tail extended from the base of his spine, ending in a tri-tipped axe-shaped structure. Ridges jutted up from his sternum, lining the space where the base of his neck and his shoulders met to frame a horned head with frills on each side. [195]
His face was reminiscent of a bare skull: a sunken nose, dark circles around light blue eyes that were overseen by pronounced eyebrows, angled cheeks, and rows of lengthy pointed teeth. Small spikes lined his chin. His flesh was blue-grey in coloration, and his horns were black. His extremities grew darker in color as they extended further away from his torso: while his shoulders were blue-grey, his hands were almost dark enough to match his black claws. [195]
Molag Bal's face is quite literally plastered all over Coldharbour. Decorative seals on fortress walls and floors depict a threatening fanged grimace. [196] Sconces lit with cold fire look like stylized, simplified sculptures of his head, [197] as do the accents on some braziers [198] and bookshelves, [199] as well as the spouts on fountains. [200] [201] Flaccus Terentius describes this show of hubris rather succinctly in his account of Molag Bal's palace, a place where "everything stares back at [Molag Bal] with his own face". [9] This iconography has made its way onto the tapestries woven by his followers, [202] as well as the banners carried by his soldiers into battle. [203] Molag Bal's face is a common symbol in his worship. [204] [205]
Molag Bal is often depicted as scarce in the way of clothing by stonemasons: during the Planemeld, he opted for a loincloth adorned with skulls, tusks, and bits of metal and bone. [195] If one were to take a blade and cut into his physical form, a viscous blue substance would leak from the wound. [206]
Molag Bal is arrogant, calculating, and cares little for the welfare of mortalkind. [36] [208] He views pacifism as a form of weakness. [117] He is also fond of ostentatious and macabre displays of power, occasionally going out of his way to hold elaborate ceremonies. An example of such an event was the "first Daedric Titan unveiling and Soul Shriven impalement celebration". [209]
The God of Brutality has little patience for failure. He scorns the Daedra beneath him for falling in battle, considering their "weakness" a blemish on their reputation and deserving of recourse. [210] Independent thought is among that which Molag Bal's servants consider "weak": they believe absolute obedience to the Prince is a virtue, and indeed a point of strength. [211] Weakness is, of course, punished by pain. [79]
The Lord of Brutality finds the suffering of others rather amusing. Before it fell to Solstice during the second attempted plane-merge headed by Wormblood , Molag Bal's realm hosted a prison known as the Ashbound Hall . The Prince would whisk vampires away from their lives on Nirn seemingly at random, sealing them in the Hall so they could fight each other for his amusement. [212] [213] A prominent scholar on the study of vampires once likened the site to a Tamrielic animal fighting ring. [214] As there were no mortals in the Hall, the inhabitants could only feed on their fellow vampires to sate their thirst—doing so does not satisfy the blood-craving the same way that feeding on a live mortal would. [215] [216] To further compound his subjects' suffering, Molag Bal would occasionally transform one of these trapped vampires into a specter of sorts, trapping them in a form that craves blood, but cannot feed on it. [217]
Molag Bal is quite keen on depriving mortals of free will. To this end, Molag Bal wields a form of mind magic : he intrudes upon the minds of mortals as a form of torture. It is an invasive, agonizing experience. [218] By forcing his way into a mortal's mind, he can show them horrific visions. [219] He also does this to Daedra, particularly magically-gifted Xivkyn. These augurs share a mental link with Molag Bal, acting as vessels to carry out his dark whims. [220] This practice has unintended risks, as mortals who have had a mental connection to the Prince forced upon them can see through his eyes if they concentrate hard enough. Molag Bal can feel the presence of one who accomplishes such a feat, and he doesn't appreciate the trespass. During the Planemeld, Varen Aquilarios did just that to distract the Daedric Prince, allowing the Vestige to traverse Coldharbour unmolested. Molag Bal was enraged by Varen's presence, leaving him blind to the hero's deeds in his realm while he dealt with the former Emperor. [218]
This mode of mental binding is not limited to the torment of living mortals: Molag Bal's minions have bound unfortunate souls to mundane objects with the intent to place them near his enemies on Tamriel, enabling the Prince to spy on those who oppose him from the mortal realm. Being forced to serve as a looking glass for Molag Bal is unbearably painful for the bound spirit. [85]
Some mortals have had their force of will completely sapped as a result of Molag Bal tampering with their mind. [221] Such was the case with the infamous pirate, Captain Blackheart , whose entire crew was transformed into undead slaves. [222] [223] He was cursed when a relic the crew had plundered spoke to him, claiming to be Molag Bal. The Prince uttered a spell, transforming the crew and making them unable to disobey their Captain's orders: [224] the Captain also fell utterly under Molag Bal's control. [225] Captain Blackheart never killed the inhabitants of the ships he plundered—following the crew's enslavement, [225] Molag Bal proceeded to slaughter hundreds of innocents with Captain Blackheart's hands, tormenting him with fleeting moments of independent thought. [221]
The practice of stealing the minds of mortals is not restricted to spellcraft, as Molag Bal also accomplishes this through alchemy . The Mind-Shriven are created when mortals are forced to imbibe a poison called the Blood of Coldharbour. These individuals have their force of will stolen by the Daedric Prince, [226] and become unnaturally aggressive as a result of their transformation. [227]
As is expected of a Daedric Prince, Molag Bal can summon his Daedric minions at will. Unfortunately for those who oppose him, this means he can summon a horde of Daedric Titans. [228] Molag Bal can weaponize souls and cold fire to assault those who wrong him. [229] [230] Those who offend Molag Bal may find themselves suddenly missing important features, such as their skin. [231] Renowned Daedrologist Endemel Githendas posits that it is plausible to assume that Molag Bal may be capable of altering a victim's bone structure in pursuit of his eternal desire to inflict agony upon others. [232] He is not above turning people into ravenous undead monsters, [231] and manipulates shadows to imprison powerful servants of Meridia. [18]
There is evidence that Molag Bal harbors some appreciation for art—an ice sculpture known as Dilogene's Ice Fangs No. 4 was one of the Prince's favorite works before its untimely destruction. [233] As well, the Dremora sculptor Skivoxo was within Bal's employ in the First Era. [234] [235] During the Interregnum, antiquarians observed a torture-display piece created by the Prince, which one scholar posited was decorated for the purpose of artistic expression. [236]
The politics of Oblivion are complex. Alliances come and go, and what each Daedric Prince thinks of one another is largely unknown. However, Molag Bal has a few clearly defined relationships with other Princes, particularly Meridia. The two aren't very fond of each other. Molag Bal revels in undeath and destruction, [15] while Meridia believes that the sanctity of life energies is not to be perverted. [237] Though they both share a strong distaste for free will, [238] these fundamental beliefs are the foundation for their bitter rivalry.
The two have actively antagonized each other throughout the course of Tamrielic history: Meridia's role in halting the Planemeld is a prominent example of this. Meridia sent a city under her protection [89] into Coldharbour to host an invasion force of her own making, [239] [240] wrested control of the Fighters Guild to turn its attention toward interfering with the Planemeld, [12] and personally appeared in the heart of Molag Bal's stronghold to destroy the Planar Vortex. Meridia plotted to oppose the Planemeld for years in advance. [241]
During the same time period, Meridia foiled Molag Bal's efforts to use the Mortuum Vivicus, a relic that siphons mortal souls and is capable of unleashing a storm of undeath. [12] This would be the second time Meridia has thwarted Bal's attempts to release the Vivicus upon Tamriel, the first occurring in the First Era during the time of Abagarlas and Delodiil . Molag Bal's worshippers plotted to harvest the souls of Meridia's faithful and unleash the Mortuum Vivicus' necromantic potential, [242] and the Lady of Light destroyed the Abagarlan's city in response. [243]
Molag Bal became so familiar with Meridia's methods during their years of conflict that he had a facility constructed specifically to imprison her servants. [26] Some ancient Khajiiti tales suggest that Meridia and Molag Bal once fought for a common goal during Bal's assault on the Lunar Lattice, [62] but for the most part, their history is rife with conflict.
Boethiah is another Prince with whom Molag Bal isn't necessarily "on good terms". This is another case of a difference in fundamental beliefs: Molag Bal expects those who worship him to obey without question. [244] On the other hand, Boethiah expects her followers to actively question and oppose authority, and despises mortals who attempt to appeal to her with bent knees and words of adoration. [245] Molag Bal seeks strength for himself, while Boethiah looks for strength in others. [246] Their differences aren't the only thing setting them apart; the two Princes have some record of antagonizing one another. In the past, Boethiah has sent her followers to desecrate Molag Bal's holy sites, and Molag Bal has retaliated in kind. [25]
Molag Bal is apparently bitter rivals with Mehrunes Dagon , and the two will thwart each other at every given opportunity. [128] The Order of the Waking Flame alleges that Mehrunes Dagon once personally freed a group of his faithful from imprisonment in Coldharbour by sending a projection of himself to do battle against Molag Bal while the faithful were transported to the Deadlands . [247] Malacath views Molag Bal's desire for adulation as unnecessary, interpreting it as a weakness. [248] The servants of Molag Bal and Azura have also thwarted each other in the past. The most noteworthy of such instances revolved around a priestess of Azura who brokered peace between two groups in Valenwood whom Molag Bal's minions were sent to divide, foiling his plan. [249] [250] [35]
Molag Bal doesn't just have conflict with other Princes, but the Divines as well. Molag Bal has a particular distaste for Arkay, who is invoked when the souls and bodies of deceased mortals are consecrated for burial. Molag Bal delights in the art of necromancy, and Arkay's opposition to the undead contradicts Molag Bal's belief that corpses are tools for him to use to further his goals. [93] In the legend of the first vampire's creation, Molag Bal invents a new form of undeath to spite Arkay's sway over the cycle of life and death. [69] [119] [251] While Arkay and Molag Bal never engage each other personally, their servants are likely to find themselves at odds with one another. The Order of Arkay is an organization that seeks to protect the autonomy of mortal souls and prevent their misuse by necromancers and Daedra. They oppose the necromantic energies wielded by Bal's cultists and the very existence of vampires. [252] Additionally, Arkay's blessing is capable of preventing mortal souls from being perverted by Molag Bal's dark influence. [253] [254]
The followers of Stendarr have also opposed the God of Schemes. Cardinal Belforte of the Order of Stendarr devoted his life to ridding Tamriel of Daedric corruption, and he was especially thorough in his persecution of Molag Bal's faithful. He acquired numerous tracts from the cults his order extinguished, which furthered scholars' understanding of the Daedric Prince's motivations. [65]
The Crown of Bones was created by Molag Bal for the Admiral of the Lost Fleet. [255] [256] It was a crown that allowed the wearer to control the skeletons of sailors whose ships were dragged into the Maelstrom of Bal. [151]
The Dolorous Cista was a vessel that harvested mortal suffering, converting it to magical energy. [257] [258] Physical pain, [259] [260] fear, [261] and frustration are but a few examples of valid sensations that the cista was capable of feeding on. [262] The relic could be filled with the suffering of both Daedra and mortals. [263] The cista showed great potential as a weapon of mass destruction: during the Interregnum , its Daedric handlers were beset by infighting over the subject of who was truly "worthy" to wield its power. [257] [258] One of the Daedric torturers laboring within the Cage speculated that the Lord of Brutality himself had plans to wield it. [258]
The Gift of Death was a daedric relic associated with both Molag Bal and Meridia . Its most known appearance was an ornate lamp that was surrounded by a dark green energy. [264] The relic had the ability to resurrect someone in return for a willing sacrifice of another individual. It was said that in return, the sacrifice's soul could not be recovered. [265] The Gift of Death was originally a Light of Meridia but had been corrupted by necrotic magic through unknown means. [266] It was first discovered in the Daedric temple of Mor Naril during the First Era . [265]
During the Worm Cult 's resurgence and operations on Solstice , the Gift's recovery was one of the main goals of Wormblood . It was to be used for the resurrection of Mannimarco , but it was instead used by a mortally injured Gabrielle Benele to bring back Darien Gautier . This act purified the Gift, restoring the Light to its original function. [267]
The Mace of Molag Bal , also known as the Vampire's Mace, is a Daedric artifact attributed to Molag Bal , the Lord of Domination and Enslavement. Its enchantment drains the stamina and magicka of its victims and transfers them to the bearer. It also has been known to have the ability to transfer an enemy's strength to its wielder or trap their soul. It has been said to be a good weapon of choice for vanquishing wizards. [268] [269]
Many legends surround the mace, but its origins can be traced back to when Molag Bal deceived an Orcish blacksmith and enslaved him in the pits of Coldharbour . The blacksmith was turned into a Soul Shriven and forced to forge the mace. [UOL 4] [270]
Due to Molag Bal's penchant for meddling in mortal affairs, he grants the mace to those he deems worthy but has also been quite free with his artifact. Molag Bal assumes that when such a powerful artifact falls into mortal hands, a trail of death and destruction is sure to follow. [268] [UOL 4] Some believe the mace to be an object of Daedra worship. [116] Molag Bal's followers refer to it as the Master's Mace, [271] [272] and often swear by it. [273]
The Mortuum Vivicus was a powerful weapon created by the Daedric Prince Molag Bal . It was a massive spell that was capable of holding and harnessing thousands of souls, and could destroy all of Tamriel if it were released. [274] It had no physical form, instead appearing as a large orb of cold light. It was originally given as a gift to the Ayleid King Anumaril of Abagarlas , a Daedraphile settlement on City Isle that was dedicated to Bal.
Harvest Hearts are artifacts that Molag Bal uses to enslave large communities and empower specific parts of his divine portfolio. [275]
During the Planemeld, the Vestige destroyed a Heart within a portion of Coldharbour known as the Orchard, a large graveyard full of vampires. That particular heart greatly increased the rate at which vampirism spreads, and turned a few of the Coldharbour invasion force's own into bloodfiends. While that Heart was intact, the vampires in the Orchard were completely under Molag Bal's control, unable to disobey their orders to protect the area. [276]
The Rings of Blood Magic are a set of two ancient enchanted rings which enhance the wearer's Blood Magic and vampiric powers. [277] The Ring of the Erudite fortifies the wearer's magicka, [278] while the Ring of the Beast fortifies the wearer's health and increases the power of a Vampire Lord 's claws. [279] [280] Both rings are triple-banded with two gemstone insets; the Ring of the Erudite is brassy and possesses green gems, while the Ring of the Beast is golden with red gems. [278] [279] [280]
According to myth, Molag Bal himself gave these rings to ancient and powerful Vampire Lords. [277]
The seven Stones of Cold Fire are artifacts that were gifted directly from Molag Bal and distributed throughout Daedric ruins on Vvardenfell that were dedicated to the Lord of Lies. These stones hold the power to summon and compel Daedra. They were used during the Interregnum by Mistress Dratha to make a deal with a Dremora named Xykenaz to prolong her life. The stones were located at Ashalmawia , Bal Ur , Esutanamus , Kushtashpi , Ramimilk , Yansirramus , and Tusenend . [281] [282]
The stones have a polished black surface and are cold to the touch. Blue flames swirl within them. [283]
Vampiric Shards are foul relics that corrupt whatever they come in contact with. When the Saxhleel village of Haj Uxith was brought to Coldharbour, Molag Bal gave the Argonians one of these crystals to keep their Hist alive; it was the only thing sustaining the Hist in Coldharbour's inhospitable soil. Over time, the shard poisoned the Hist. The Hist was dying, but the shard kept it from passing, forcing it to endure constant torment. [284]
Coldharbour is a realm of Oblivion created and ruled over by Molag Bal , the Daedric Prince of Domination. As befits his sphere, Bal's realm is a desolate, apocalyptic image of Nirn , which is achieved by both mocking imitation and outright theft of parts of the mortal realm. It is inhabited by Daedra and the eternally-tormented husks of mortals called Soul Shriven . [285]
Mortals who made deals with Molag Bal can also be found living in Coldharbour. [286] [287] The souls of vampires are sent to Coldharbour when they die. [288] [289] [290] Coldharbour has been described as a manifestation of fear and exploitation. [291] Descriptions of the plane vary widely, but all accounts agree that Coldharbour is a dismal, cold, and largely lifeless realm. [285]
Coldharbour is full of innumerable facilities, all built to further Molag Bal's dark schemes. Among these facilities is the Library of Dusk , a library filled with propaganda that serves as a trap for scholars. [292] It is available for use by Molag Bal's minions. [293] There is another library in Coldharbour that is slightly less accessible—it is Molag Bal's personal collection, located in his stronghold. The shelves in Molag Bal's library span entire cliffs and run deep into a chasm. Unsurprisingly, plenty of the books on his shelves are very disturbing. [294]
The realm possesses several qualities that function as security measures. Things that teleport into Coldharbour will find themselves scattered, ending up in locations they did not intend to land. [295] [296] Magical constructs known as Coldharbour Sentinels are used by Molag Bal to guide his vision in Coldharbour. With their aid, he can see into whatever prison they're anchored in. These Sentinels are magically connected, so destroying one will blind the other Sentinels it was bound to. [297]
Coldharbour mostly consists of prisons, but other facilities include those where research is conducted. He is fascinated by the metamagical technology of soul gems, and often has a mystical research project or two in the works to develop new and more efficient methods of stealing and imprisoning the souls of mortals. [298] Not every part of Coldharbour serves a practical use; there are many graveyards in Molag Bal's plane of Oblivion for no other reason than because he likes them. [299] The giant skeletal structures in the Ossein Cage are intended to instill a sense of horror and dread in those who walk its fractured valleys. [232]
Molag Bal maintains a strong military within his realm. He expects complete loyalty from his servants, and does not tolerate weakness among his ranks. [300] [301] [244] Daedra most commonly seen in his service include Dremora , Clannfear , Daedroth , [97] Grievous Twilights , Daedric Titans , Cold-Flame Atronachs , Banekin and Harvesters . Ogrim , Scamps , Watchers , Winged Twilights , Xivilai , Spider Daedra and Dark Seducers are also found in his service. The Xivkyn are a Daedric race that is exclusively loyal to Molag Bal, and serve as his personal guard . [162] They behave much like their master; they constantly scheme against one another and they obsessively collect soul gems. [7] [302] The hooved Maldrith are also counted among his ranks. [303] [UOL 5] They serve Molag Bal as roaming inspectors and inquisitors, and are feared by other Daedra. [304]
Molag Bal forbade ordinary Flame Atronachs from being summoned in his realm after a subordinate conjured one in his palace and damaged his favorite ice sculpture. Their presence within Coldharbour was eventually replaced by Cold-Flame Atronachs. [233]
The Dremora within his service are divided into several clans, the most prominent and powerful being the Deathbringers and the Foolkillers , who struggle against each other for prominence within Molag Bal's ranks. [305] During the Interregnum, the Deathbringers were at the forefront of this struggle. [306] Their Valkynaz was a trusted servant of Molag Bal who was tasked with guarding the key to the Prince's inner sanctum and had access to the Daedric Prince himself. [307] Three other Dremora are known to have been part of Molag Bal's inner council. [308] Aside from playing a part in Coldharbour's politics, they fill the ranks as soldiers, couriers, guards, and torturers, among other roles. [309] [310] The Darkbinders are a low-ranking Dremora clan that serve as jailers within Coldharbour. Darkbinders are occasionally assigned to administer punishment to soul shriven who are of particular interest to Molag Bal. [311] After the Planemeld, an up and coming group of Dremora known as the Soulbinders sought to form their own clan to fill a power vacuum left by the Deathbringers. [312]
It is a fairly simple task to discern a minion of Molag Bal from those in service to another Prince: the easiest way to tell is by looking at their eyes. The eyes of Daedra within Molag Bal's service tend to be icy blue, just like their masters', and many of them have blue or blue-grey skin. [313] [314] It is believed to be because Molag Bal likes to twist things into his own image. [UOL 5] Stonefire Scamps are particularly easy to discern as servants of Molag Bal, as their flesh and fur are a rich blue-grey, their eyes are the same tone as their master's and they have deep black horns. [315] Daedrats and Fiendroths can be seen scuttling around in Coldharbour and in places on Mundus where Molag Bal's influence is pervasive. [316]
Slate-Skinned Daedrats are a breed whose distinct markings and blue eyes stem from their diet of corpses that have been touched by the Lord of Brutality. While not brazen minions of Molag Bal, they bear characteristics common in his servants, and are often studied by those interested in researching or thwarting the Prince. [317]
The rules that govern Molag Bal's minions are outlined in two known doctrines: the Seven-Hundred-and-One Edicts is the primary set of rules, and the Mandatory Codicils cover special cases. Breaking one of these rules lands the offending party a sentence to a facility known as the Scathe-Rings, where those imprisoned can be sentenced to many excruciating punishments. [318] One such punishment is "Second-Degree Gradual Discorporation". [26] Strict uniform codes are among the guidelines that govern Bal's servants. [30] [80] Those whose service is exceptional (i.e.: exceeding quotas in certain realms of employment) may earn themselves time in the Bliss-Cells. [13]
Daedra are not the only ones rewarded in Coldharbour's work culture. Soul-Shriven slaves who inform on their compatriots can be rewarded with shifts of lighter labor or a visit to the Grotto of Depravity, [319] a cave that serves as the site of an eternal party. This doesn't mean the shriven get to have fun: there's a high probability that the Daedra who are actually enjoying themselves will torment the soulless husks in some manner, and shriven suffering is often mandated. [320] Despite this, the shriven assigned to the grotto are permitted brief reprieve, even fishing in the plasm pools under constant Daedric supervision. [321]
If one of Molag Bal's servants is "put on trial" for their transgressions, they are tried in a facility known as the Court of Contempt , where they are entitled to a counsel of Scamps outfitted in puffy wigs. Scamps are not allowed to speak in the Court of Contempt, and the Court has a one-hundred-percent conviction rate of defendants, otherwise known as "the Guilty". These trials are mostly for show and never end fairly. [322] [323] One notable instance of a minion incurring the Daedric Prince's wrath is an incident known as "The Seat of Tyranny Rude Cushion Incident", wherein a Dremora named Xykenaz was imprisoned in a nanoplane for seven epochs for putting a whoopie-cushion on Molag Bal's throne. [324]
Molag Bal has appointed quite a few powerful servants to serve as generals or other high-ranking officers. Among these servants are spies, [325] agents of chaos , brutal prison wardens , and gifted mages . [244] [20] Molag Bal values cunning as well as strength, and seems to grant power to those he finds interesting or strong... even if their claim to fame is upsetting the balance of power among the other Daedra within his service. Though he would prefer his military hierarchy remains stable, he has rewarded servants of his for displays of guile in the past. [244] [127] [326] Molag Bal has also employed a scribe named Denogorath , whose full title is "Dread Archivist and Promulgator of Mandatory Doctrine". [327]
Mortals also serve the Daedric Prince in his realm. Apart from the cultists that worship him on Nirn, Molag Bal has the Soul-Shriven and Mind-Shriven as slaves, with the Dremora as their common overseers. [328] Vampires may also be found serving the Daedric Prince. [329] [330]
The extent that the Seven-Hundred-and-One Edicts applies to mortals is not known, but there are consequences for mortals who displease Molag Bal nonetheless. Agents who disappoint the Daedric Prince harshly enough are pitted against each other in an endless war at the Cliffs of Failure . The basic premise of the "game" at the Cliffs is for the participants to fight to win back the Dark Lord's favor. [84] However, there is no end to the game, and these individuals are doomed to fight for Molag Bal's amusement forever. [113] Mortals and Daedra who anger Molag Bal severely are tormented in the vestibule of Heart's Grief . [331] These individuals have usually failed the Daedric Prince [332] or committed some other egregious infraction. [333]
